ಕಾರ್ಯವಿಧಾನ / ಸಂಶೋಧನೆಗಳು: ರೋಗಿಯು ಬಲ ಮುಂಗೈಯಲ್ಲಿ 4x3 cm ಗಾತ್ರದ ಗಟ್ಟಿಯಾದ, ಚಲಿಸುವ ಗಡ್ಡೆಯೊಂದಿಗೆ 3 ತಿಂಗಳ ಇತಿಹಾಸದೊಂದಿಗೆ ಹೆಚ್ಚುತ್ತಿರುವ ಊತ ಮತ್ತು ಸಾಂದರ್ಭಿಕ ನೋವಿನೊಂದಿಗೆ ಬಂದಿದ್ದರು. ಇಮೇಜಿಂಗ್ ವಿವಿಧ ರೀತಿಯ ಮೃದು ಅಂಗಾಂಶದ ಗಡ್ಡೆಯೊಂದಿಗೆ ನೆಕ್ರೋಸಿಸ್ ಮತ್ತು ಕ್ಯಾಲ್ಸಿಫಿಕೇಶನ್ ಪ್ರದೇಶಗಳನ್ನು ತೋರಿಸಿದೆ. ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ಸಮಯದಲ್ಲಿ ಫ್ರೀಜ್ ಮಾಡಿದ ಸೆಕ್ಷನ್ ಹೈ-ಗ್ರೇಡ್ ಸಾರ್ಕೋಮಾವನ್ನು ದೃಢಪಡಿಸಿದೆ. 2 cm ಅಂಚುಗಳೊಂದಿಗೆ ವ್ಯಾಪಕ ಸ್ಥಳೀಯ ಛೇದನ ಮಾಡಲಾಯಿತು. ನರನಾಳೀಯ ರಚನೆಗಳನ್ನು ಸಂರಕ್ಷಿಸಲಾಗಿದೆ. ಮಾದರಿಯನ್ನು ಅಂಚಿನ ಮೌಲ್ಯಮಾಪನಕ್ಕಾಗಿ ಕಳುಹಿಸಲಾಗಿದೆ. ಅಂದಾಜು ರಕ್ತದ ನಷ್ಟ 150 ಮಿಲಿ. ರೋಗಿಯು ಪ್ರಕ್ರಿಯೆಯನ್ನು ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಸಹಿಸಿಕೊಂಡರು. ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರದ ಯೋಜನೆಯು ಗಾಯದ ಆರೈಕೆ, ಫಿಸಿಯೋಥೆರಪಿ ಮತ್ತು ಅಡ್ಜುವೆಂಟ್ ಥೆರಪಿಗಾಗಿ ಆಂಕೊಲಾಜಿ ರೆಫರಲ್ ಅನ್ನು ಒಳಗೊಂಡಿದೆ. [[BED_NUMBER|15]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-0023]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-88912]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Siddaraju Kumar]]

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Hugli.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Telemarketing Sales Person
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Hugli
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Jogendra Das, a 35-year-old male, is a telemarketing sales person from rural West Bengal, currently married, with below primary education. His first language is Bengali, and he works in a structured, target-driven role. He lives in the Hugli district and follows traditional Bengali customs, with a modest lifestyle and basic literacy.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: An ambitious fashion professional, Gopal Baladar leverages his pattern‑making expertise, basic CAD skills, and deep appreciation for Karnataka’s handloom traditions to design sustainable garments, while his organized approach and courteous communication with regional suppliers ensure smooth order fulfillment.
payload.linguistic_persona: A multilingual communicator, Gopal Baladar speaks fluent Kannada, conversational Telugu, functional English for technical work, and understands basic Hindi, allowing him to bridge local artisans and broader market partners.
payload.sports_persona: A weekend hiker and cricket enthusiast, Gopal Baladar follows the Karnataka Ranji team, roots for Bengaluru FC in the ISL, and regularly treks the Kabini hills to stay fit, though he often skips gym sessions in favor of outdoor pursuits.
payload.arts_persona: A cultural aficionado, Gopal Baladar frequently attends Yakshagana shows by the Chandramukhi troupe, practices mridangam to the rhythm of M. Balamuralikrishna’s Carnatic renditions, sketches fashion concepts while being inspired by the photography of Prasad K. V., and enjoys the folk beats of Dollu Kunitha during village festivals.
payload.travel_persona: A budget‑conscious explorer, Gopal Baladar enjoys weekend trips to Bengaluru for design expos, yearly pilgrimages to Mysore during Dasara, occasional escapes to the serene backwaters of Kerala, and dreams of a future fashion‑inspired pilgrimage to Paris, always planning trips around festival calendars and affordable train fares.
payload.culinary_persona: A home‑cooking enthusiast, Gopal Baladar loves whipping up bisi bele bath with freshly ground sesame oil and tamarind pulp, perfects ragi mudde for a hearty breakfast, and often savors a steaming plate of masala dosa with coconut chutney, while he occasionally experiments with fusion toppings like cheese and jalapeño.
payload.persona: A curious, organized 23‑year‑old fashion designer, Gopal Baladar blends rural handloom heritage with modern sustainability, while juggling a love for mridangam beats, weekend treks, and an occasional caffeine‑driven late‑night sketch session.
payload.cultural_background: Gopal grows up in the rural hinterland of Hassan district in Karnataka. His family belongs to the Kannada-speaking agrarian community, steeped in local festivals like Ugadi, Sankranti, and the vibrant folk traditions of Yakshagana and Dollu Kunitha. The proximity to neighboring Andhra Pradesh brings a Telugu cultural influence in family ties and cuisine. The region’s strong Hindu ethos, with daily visits to the local Shiva temple and participation in village fairs, shapes his worldview. The modest rural setting instills a sense of practical resourcefulness (jugaad) while exposing him to the rich handloom heritage of the state.
payload.linguistic_background: Gopal's primary language is Kannada, which he speaks fluently and reads and writes with native proficiency. He learned Telugu at home from relatives and is comfortable conversing and reading Telugu script, though his writing is modest. His technical diploma involved instruction in English, giving him functional proficiency for reading manuals, drafting pattern notes, and communicating with suppliers; he can hold everyday conversations in English but may mix in Kannada. Hindi exposure is limited to school basics; he can understand simple spoken Hindi and read Devanagari signs, but he is not confident writing in Hindi.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Gopal's core expertise lies in the fashion and garment sector. He is adept at pattern making, fabric selection, and garment fitting, ensuring that dresses meet both aesthetic and functional standards. He conducts quality inspections and trend analysis to keep collections relevant. His technical diploma equipped him with basic CAD (Computer-Aided Design) for fashion, enabling him to draft simple design sketches digitally. Beyond technical skills, Gopal exhibits strong organizational planning, inventory management, and reliable customer service, often coordinating orders and deliveries for local boutiques. Multilingual communication in Kannada and Telugu enhances his ability to liaise with regional suppliers.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Pattern Making','Fabric Selection','Garment Fitting','Quality Inspection','Trend Analysis','Basic CAD for Fashion','Organizational Planning','Inventory Management','Customer Service','Multilingual Communication (Kannada, Telugu)']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Gopal enjoys exploring Karnataka’s folk arts such as Yakshagana and Dollu Kunitha, often attending local performances. He listens to Carnatic music and practices playing the mridangam on weekends. Sketching fashion ideas and experimenting with fabric draping are his creative outlets. Photography, especially capturing rural landscapes and textile details, keeps him engaged. He loves trekking in the Kabini hills, cooking traditional dishes like bisi bele bath, and volunteering at village festivals to help organize cultural programs.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Yakshagana Performances','Dollu Kunitha Dance','Listening to Carnatic Music','Playing Mridangam','Fashion Sketching','Fabric Draping Experiments','Photography (Rural Landscapes, Textile Details)','Trekking in Kabini Hills','Cooking Traditional Dishes (Bisi Bele Bath)','Volunteering at Village Festivals']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Gopal aims to evolve from a dress diver into a senior fashion designer specializing in sustainable, regionally-inspired garments. Over the next five years, he plans to enroll in a diploma in fashion design, master advanced CAD tools, and acquire certification in eco‑friendly textile practices. He aspires to launch his own boutique in Hassan that showcases handloom fabrics from Karnataka and blends them with contemporary designs, thereby creating employment opportunities for local artisans. Ultimately, he envisions his brand becoming a recognized name for ethical fashion across South India.
payload.education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 23‑year‑old male from rural Hassan, Karnataka, works as a dress diver with a technical diploma, is never married, and speaks Kannada as his primary language. He follows a lifestyle that includes weekend treks, occasional caffeine‑driven sketching, and a diet rich in traditional dishes like bisi bele bath and ragi mudde. His background includes a cultural appreciation for local festivals and folk arts, with a professional focus on sustainable handloom design.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]

- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Patient: આભાર ડૉક્ટર. હું આવતીકાલે હોસ્પિટલમાં જઈશ. શું તમે મને ક્ષય રોગની દવાઓ વિશે વધુ જણાવી શકો છો? શું તેની આડઅસરો થશે? સાથે જ, મારા બ્લડ પ્રેશર માટે મારે શું કરવું જોઈએ?

--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste Doctor. I have been having a persistent cough for the past three weeks, and it's getting worse. Sometimes I cough up blood-tinged sputum. I also feel very tired all the time and have lost some weight without trying. I have a mild fever in the evenings. Additionally, I have been feeling dizzy and my hands sometimes shake, which I think might be related to my high blood pressure. I am a construction worker and I live in a crowded area in Bharuch.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Shah]]: Hello Batukbhai. Thank you for sharing your symptoms. The persistent cough with blood-tinged sputum, weight loss, fatigue, and evening fever are concerning for tuberculosis. The dizziness and shaking could be related to your blood pressure. I would like to recommend a few tests to confirm the diagnosis and check your blood pressure. Please visit the District Hospital Bharuch tomorrow for a sputum test and a blood pressure check. Also, start taking the following medications: Paracetamol for fever, and I will prescribe an anti-tuberculosis regimen once the tests confirm TB. Please avoid going to work until you are evaluated.

Patient: Thank you, Doctor. I will go to the hospital tomorrow. Can you tell me more about the anti-tuberculosis medications? Will they have side effects? Also, what should I do about my blood pressure?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Shah]]: The anti-tuberculosis medications are usually a combination of four drugs: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol. They are given for two months, followed by two drugs (Isoniazid and Rifampicin) for four months. Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, and yellowing of urine. If you experience severe side effects, contact me immediately. For your blood pressure, we will start with a low dose of an antihypertensive medication and monitor your response. Please avoid salty foods and try to reduce stress. We will also arrange for a follow-up call in a week to check on your progress.

Patient: I understand, Doctor. I will be careful with the medications and follow up. Thank you for your help.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Shah]]: You're welcome, Batukbhai. Remember to take the medications as prescribed and complete the full course. If you have any questions or concerns, don't hesitate to contact us. We are here to support you. Take care and get well soon.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle

- district: Kokrajhar

Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

**सुबुरुन:**
1.  डटस (DOTS) होगिरिजों होनाय बायदियै ए.टि.टि.नि गासै कर्सखौ सोलिबाय था। आद्रा थानाय मुलिफोरखौ जायगानि गुदि सावस्रि मिरुआव मोननो हागोन।

4.  बिथांनि फिसाय, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jiran Boro]], खौ फाहामथाइ मानिनायनि गोनांथिखौ फोरमाय।

payload.generated_text_en:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kokrajhar Civil Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Boro]] DOB [[DOB|2004-08-15]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Kalita]] | Course / advice: Patient is a 20-year-old homemaker from Kokrajhar, admitted with a 3-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Investigations revealed a positive GeneXpert test for Mycobacterium tuberculosis and a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe on chest X-ray. She was started on the standard Category I anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) regimen. During her 2-week inpatient stay, she responded well to treatment, becoming afebrile and reporting significant improvement in her cough and appetite. She is being discharged in a stable condition.

**Advice:**
1.  Continue the full course of ATT as prescribed by the DOTS provider. The remaining medication is available at the local Primary Health Centre.
2.  Maintain a nutritious diet, including protein-rich foods like lentils and eggs, to support recovery.
3.  Attend all scheduled follow-up appointments at the District TB Centre for monitoring and medication refills.
4.  Inform her husband, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jiran Boro]], about the importance of treatment adherence.
5.  A baseline screening for diabetes mellitus is recommended during her next visit to monitor for any non-communicable disease comorbidity.
6.  For any queries, contact the hospital at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].
7.  Her ABHA ID is [[ABHA_ADDRESS|rina.boro@abdm]] for digital health records.
8.  Patient belongs to the [[CASTE|ST]] community and resides in [[DISTRICT|Kokrajhar]].

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Purnia.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Carpenter, Construction
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Purnia
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 35-year-old male carpenter from rural Purnia, Bihar, who is married and literate in Hindi. He works in construction and is currently employed as a carpenter. He is a resident of the rural zone in the state of Bihar. He is a Hindi speaker. He has a habit of nail-biting and anxiety. He is a fan of cricket and enjoys listening to Bhojpuri folk music. He enjoys rustic fare such as litti-chokha and ginger-masala chai. He is a small-scale contractor.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Lilavati Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Patel]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Priya Deshmukh]], [[AGE|44]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Mumbai Suburban]]
Reason: Persistent breast lump with suspicious imaging findings requiring oncological evaluation

Dear Colleague,

I am referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Priya Deshmukh]], a 44-year-old female homemaker from Mumbai Suburban, for oncological assessment. The patient presents with a 3-month history of a palpable lump in the right breast, initially noticed during self-examination. The lump has progressively increased in size and is now approximately 2.5 cm in diameter.

Clinical examination reveals a firm, non-mobile mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast with irregular borders. No axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable. Mammography performed at our facility shows a suspicious spiculated lesion with microcalcifications. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was inconclusive, showing atypical epithelial cells.

The patient has no significant family history of breast or ovarian cancer. She is nulliparous and has never been pregnant. Her menarche was at age 13, and menopause has not yet occurred. She reports regular menstrual cycles.

Past medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled on medication. She has no known diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Her BMI is 26.5 kg/m².

The patient is emotionally distressed about the diagnosis and requires comprehensive oncological evaluation including possible biopsy, staging studies, and multidisciplinary management planning.

For further queries, please contact me at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] and [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Deshmukh]] (father) are available for any additional information.

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH012024012345]] are available for identification purposes.

Sincerely,
Dr. Anjali Patel
Department of General Medicine
Lilavati Hospital, Mumbai

Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Priya Deshmukh]], [[AGE|44]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Mumbai Suburban]]
Reason: Persistent breast lump with suspicious imaging findings requiring oncological evaluation

Dear Colleague,

I am referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Priya Deshmukh]], a 44-year-old female homemaker from Mumbai Suburban, for oncological assessment. The patient presents with a 3-month history of a palpable lump in the right breast, initially noticed during self-examination. The lump has progressively increased in size and is now approximately 2.5 cm in diameter.

Clinical examination reveals a firm, non-mobile mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast with irregular borders. No axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable. Mammography performed at our facility shows a suspicious spiculated lesion with microcalcifications. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was inconclusive, showing atypical epithelial cells.

The patient has no significant family history of breast or ovarian cancer. She is nulliparous and has never been pregnant. Her menarche was at age 13, and menopause has not yet occurred. She reports regular menstrual cycles.

Past medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled on medication. She has no known diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Her BMI is 26.5 kg/m².

The patient is emotionally distressed about the diagnosis and requires comprehensive oncological evaluation including possible biopsy, staging studies, and multidisciplinary management planning.

For further queries, please contact me at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] and [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Deshmukh]] (father) are available for any additional information.

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH012024012345]] are available for identification purposes.

Sincerely,
Dr. Anjali Patel
Department of General Medicine
Lilavati Hospital, Mumbai

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:

He plans to enrol in a government‑run skill development programme to obtain a formal certification, which will enable him to bid for larger construction contracts. By hiring and training two apprentices, he hopes to expand his team, increase productivity, and achieve a steady income that alleviates his financial worries and reduces his anxiety. Ultimately, he wishes to be recognised as a reliable local contractor while maintaining a balanced family life.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 35-year-old male carpenter from rural Purnia, Bihar, who is married and literate in Hindi. He works in construction and is currently married with no formal education beyond literacy. His lifestyle includes nail-biting during quiet moments and late-night DIY video binges. He speaks Hindi and is culturally influenced by rural Bihar customs.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Purnia.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Carpenter, Construction
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Purnia
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 35-year-old male carpenter from rural Purnia, Bihar, who is married and literate in Hindi. He works in construction and is currently married with no formal education beyond literacy. His lifestyle includes nail-biting during quiet moments and late-night DIY video binges. He speaks Hindi and is culturally influenced by rural Bihar customs.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

रोगी [[PATIENT_NAME|रोगी का नाम]], [[AGE|35]] / [[GENDER|Male]], मेडिकल रिकॉर्ड नंबर [[MRN|RX-2024-001]]

payload.professional_persona: Geeta Devi is a seasoned bee‑keeper who runs a disciplined hive operation, trains younger women, negotiates market rates with savvy, and spearheads community workshops, balancing curiosity for new organic methods with proven traditional practices.
payload.linguistic_persona: Geeta Devi speaks fluent Maithili and Hindi, relying on a sharp ear and memorised folk verses to communicate, though she cannot read or write either script.
payload.sports_persona: Geeta Devi follows Indian cricket passionately, cheering for the national team and the local Madhubani Cricket Club whenever matches are held at the village ground, though she never plays herself.
payload.arts_persona: Geeta Devi participates in Madhubani painting festivals, admires legendary painter Sita Devi, enjoys Maithili folk songs by Sharda Sinha, and plays the dholak during celebrations, often quoting verses from poet Vidyapati.
payload.travel_persona: Geeta Devi enjoys weekend trips to Varanasi for spiritual immersion and occasional trips to Delhi’s honey market, traveling by bus with family, preferring familiar routes over distant adventures.
payload.culinary_persona: Geeta Devi loves Litti Chokha, Sattu Paratha, and adds freshly harvested honey to her tea and desserts, favoring mustard greens, mustard oil, and roasted cumin for flavor.
payload.persona: Geeta Devi is a disciplined bee‑keeper who blends old‑world charm with a competitive drive, loves folk songs, and never misses a cricket match.
payload.cultural_background: Geeta hails from the Maithil community in Madhubani, Bihar, a region famed for its rich folk traditions, Madhubani paintings, and agrarian way of life. Growing up in a rural setting, she is deeply rooted in the local customs, seasonal festivals, and community networks. Her family follows a blend of traditional Hindu rituals and Maithili folk practices, which shape her values of hard work, hospitality, and reverence for nature – especially the bees that are integral to the region's ecosystem.
payload.linguistic_background: Geeta's mother tongue is Maithili, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with family and neighbours. She is also comfortable conversing in Hindi, the lingua franca of Bihar, especially during market transactions and community gatherings. Owing to her illiteracy, she cannot read or write Maithili or Hindi scripts, but she adeptly navigates oral traditions, memorised folk songs and verbal instructions, relying on practical experience and strong auditory skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Geeta is an experienced bee-keeper with deep practical knowledge of hive construction, seasonal colony management, and honey extraction using traditional tools. Her disciplined approach ensures regular monitoring of bee health, optimal feeding, and disease prevention. She excels in organizing the processing and packaging of honey, negotiating market prices at local haats, and training younger women in beekeeping techniques. Her sociable nature makes her a natural community leader, capable of coordinating collective efforts, arranging workshops, and leading village meetings.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Bee-keeping', 'Hive construction', 'Seasonal colony management', 'Honey extraction', 'Honey processing and packaging', 'Market negotiation', 'Training and mentorship', 'Community leadership', 'Workshop organization']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Geeta loves listening to Maithili folk songs and participating in local cultural events such as the Madhubani painting festivals and village fairs. She enjoys gardening, particularly growing flowering plants that attract bees. In her spare time she engages in storytelling (kathas), cooking traditional Bihari dishes, and organizing communal gatherings where she often shares honey‑based recipes. She also likes playing the dholak during festive occasions and watching cricket matches at the village ground.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Maithili folk songs', 'Madhubani painting festivals', 'Village fairs', 'Gardening', 'Storytelling (kathas)', 'Cooking traditional Bihari dishes', 'Organizing community gatherings', 'Playing the dholak', 'Watching cricket matches']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Geeta aims to expand her beekeeping operation by increasing the number of hives to 100 within the next five years, introducing organic honey products, and establishing a cooperative with neighbouring bee‑keepers to secure better market access. She aspires to mentor more women in the village, creating a sustainable livelihood model that blends traditional practices with modest modern improvements. Additionally, she wishes to host an annual 'Madhumakkhi Mahotsav' in Madhubani to celebrate the importance of pollinators and promote community health.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Geeta Devi, a 55-year-old female bee-keeper from Madhubani, Bihar, is currently married and has an illiterate education level. She resides in a rural area and speaks Maithili as her first language, with basic Hindi proficiency. Geeta's lifestyle includes traditional beekeeping practices and a diet rich in local produce like honey, mustard greens, and Sattu.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Madhubani.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 55
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Bee-keeper
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Madhubani
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Geeta Devi, a 55-year-old female bee-keeper from Madhubani, Bihar, is currently married and has an illiterate education level. She resides in a rural area and speaks Maithili as her first language, with basic Hindi proficiency. Geeta's lifestyle includes traditional beekeeping practices and a diet rich in local produce like honey, mustard greens, and Sattu.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Goa, district=South Goa.

Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
