मुख्य तक्रार: सतत थकवा आनी दाव्या हाताच्या कोपराचेर एक नवी, न भरणारी जखम.

वर्तमान आजाराचा इतिहास:
रुग्ण 24 वर्सांची गृहिणी आसा, जिच्या कडेन तीन सप्तकांचो सतत थकवा आनी दाव्या हाताच्या कोपराचेर नवी, रंगद्रव्य असलेली, अनियमित आकाराची जखम आसा. जखम न भरणारी आसा, केन्ना केन्नाय रक्तस्त्राव जाता, आनी तिचो आकार वाडला. तिका ताप, वजन कमी जावप, वा रात्री घाम येवप हांचो न्हयकार आसा. तिच्या बापाय कडेन उच्च रक्तदाबाचो कौटुंबिक इतिहास आसा. घराच्या जबाबदाऱ्यांक लागून तिका खूब ताण येता अशें ती सांगता.

तपासणी:
सामान्य: सतर्क, शुद्धीवर, थकलेली दिसता.
जीवनावश्यक चिन्हे: रक्तदाब 130/85 mmHg, नाडी 88/min, श्वसन दर 16/min, तापमान 98.4°F.
कात: दाव्या हाताच्या कोपराच्या फाटल्या भागाचेर 1.5 cm x 1.2 cm अनियमित, बहु-रंगी (तपकिरी, काळी, तांबडी) मॅक्युलोपॅप्युलर जखम आसा, जिचे कडे अनियमित आसात आनी व्यास >6mm आसा. हेर खंयचीच कातीची जखम नोंद जाल्ली ना. उरिल्ली सगळी शरीर तपासणी सामान्य आसा.

तपासण्या:
जखमेची पंच बायोप्सी.
CBC, ESR, आनी मूत्रपिंड कार्य चाचण्यांचो आस्पाव आशिल्ली मूलभूत रक्त तपासणीचो आदेश दिला.
त्वचाशास्त्र विभागा कडेन सल्ला घेवपाचें नियोजन केलां.

योजना:
1. स्थानिक भूल दिवन दाव्या हाताच्या कोपराच्या जखमेची पंच बायोप्सी.
2. बायोप्सी ऊतकविकृतीशास्त्र तपासणी खातीर धाडप.
3. मूलभूत प्रयोगशाळा चाचण्या: CBC, ESR, RFT.
4. रुग्णाक सूर्य संरक्षण आनी स्वतःच्या कातीची तपासणी हाचेर सल्ला दिवप.
5. बायोप्सीच्या निकालां बद्दल चर्चा करपा खातीर एका सप्तकांत फुडली भेट.
6. समुपदेशन संदर्भा सयत ताण हाताळप.
payload.generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|South Goa District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SG-DH-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anita Gaonkar]] | DOB [[DOB|2000-05-22]] | Age: [[AGE|24]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-OPD-2024-0522-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Salcete]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Gaonkar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|REG-0042]] | District: [[DISTRICT|South Goa]]
Chief complaint: Persistent fatigue and a new, non-healing lesion on the left forearm.

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 24-year-old female homemaker presenting with a three-week history of progressive fatigue and a new, pigmented, irregular-shaped lesion on her left forearm. The lesion is non-healing, sometimes bleeds, and has increased in size. She denies any fever, weight loss, or night sweats. She has a family history of hypertension in her father. She reports high stress due to household responsibilities.

Examination:
General: Alert, oriented, appears fatigued. Vitals: BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, RR 16/min, Temp 98.4°F. Skin: A 1.5 cm x 1.2 cm irregular, multi-colored (brown, black, red) maculopapular lesion on the dorsal aspect of the left forearm with irregular borders and a diameter >6mm. No other skin lesions noted. Rest of the systemic examination is unremarkable.

Investigations:
Punch biopsy of the lesion. Baseline blood work including CBC, ESR, and renal function tests are ordered. A consultation with the Dermatology department is planned.

Plan:
1. Punch biopsy of the left forearm lesion under local anesthesia.
2. Send biopsy for histopathological examination.
3. Baseline labs: CBC, ESR, RFT.
4. Counsel patient on sun protection and self-skin examination.
5. Follow-up appointment in one week to discuss biopsy results.
6. Manage stress with counseling referral.
payload.judge_reasoning: Konkani Devanagari prose matches expected script/domain; all TYPEs in allow-list; age/sex/lesion fit oncology without mismatch or implausibility; Latin tags and medical terms acceptable.

payload.professional_persona: An experienced community event facilitator, Ellen Angami coordinates church festivals, cultural workshops and literacy clubs in Dimapur, applying her meticulous budgeting, multilingual translation and vibrant networking skills, and they channel their competitive spirit to deliver flawless, impactful gatherings.
payload.linguistic_persona: A multilingual communicator, Ellen Angami fluently speaks Manipuri, comfortably converses in Assamese, understands spoken English and basic Hindi, and they leverage their organized, talkative nature to act as a linguistic bridge between neighbors and extended family.
payload.sports_persona: An enthusiastic participant in local volleyball matches, Ellen Angami plays for the Dimapur community league, follows the Indian Premier League cricket fervently, and they incorporate brisk morning walks in their garden routine for fitness.
payload.arts_persona: A passionate arts enthusiast, Ellen Angami regularly attends folk‑dance showcases, admires the paintings of local tribal artists and the classical music of Manipuri violinist Koireng, and they often volunteer to organize cultural storytelling evenings at the community centre.
payload.travel_persona: An avid traveler, Ellen Angami enjoys weekend getaways to Shillong’s tea gardens, explores the historic sites of Imphal, and they dream of a pilgrimage trek to Bhutan’s monasteries, planning each trip with meticulous itineraries despite a modest budget.
payload.culinary_persona: A creative home cook, Ellen Angami delights in blending bamboo shoot, Manipuri pork with mustard, and Assamese tea leaf masala to craft signature fusion dishes, yet they admit to over‑spicing their curries when experimenting late at night.
payload.persona: Ellen Angami is a talkative, methodical community pillar who fuses tribal arts with Christian service, enjoys competitive quizzes, and never lets a missed deadline spoil their garden’s fresh harvest.
payload.cultural_background: Ellen is a 52‑year‑old Christian from Dimapur, Nagaland, who belongs to the Angami Naga community. Although her family roots are in the Manipuri‑speaking community, she has grown up amidst the multicultural tapestry of urban Nagaland where Naga tribal customs, Christian traditions, and neighboring Assamese and Manipuri cultures intersect. She participates in church gatherings, observes local Naga festivals, and values the art forms of both the Naga and Manipuri peoples.
payload.linguistic_background: Ellen’s mother‑tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she speaks fluently and reads and writes with confidence, having learned it at home and through her primary schooling. She also communicates comfortably in Assamese, which she uses daily for neighbourhood interactions and for handling basic paperwork; her reading and writing skills in Assamese are functional but not advanced. Though she did not receive formal English education beyond primary level, she can understand spoken English and manage simple conversations, especially in church and community settings. Her exposure to Hindi in the broader Indian environment gives her a rudimentary ability to comprehend and speak Hindi, but she does not read or write it fluently.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Ellen’s practical skill set centres on running an efficient household and supporting her community. She excels at budgeting, meal planning and preparing traditional Naga and Manipuri cuisine, often adapting recipes for large gatherings. Her methodical nature makes her an effective event organiser for church festivals, community fairs and family celebrations, where she coordinates logistics, decor and volunteer schedules. She acts as a linguistic bridge between Manipuri‑speaking relatives and Assamese‑speaking neighbours, facilitating simple translation and communication. Ellen also volunteers as a mentor for basic literacy, helping children and senior neighbours improve reading and writing in Manipuri and Assamese. Her keen eye for art drives her involvement in local folk‑dance workshops and cultural storytelling sessions, and she maintains a modest vegetable garden that supplies fresh produce for her family.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Budgeting and financial planning", "Traditional Naga and Manipuri cooking", "Event planning and coordination", "Community volunteering", "Basic translation (Manipuri‑Assamese)", "Literacy mentoring", "Folk art appreciation", "Cultural storytelling", "Gardening", "Conflict mediation", "Problem solving", "Basic computer usage for community tasks"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Ellen enjoys attending local art exhibitions, folk dance performances, and church music concerts. She loves experimenting with new recipes, especially fusion dishes that blend Naga, Manipuri and Assamese flavors. Her competitive streak finds an outlet in her participation in community quiz nights and bingo games where she enjoys a good challenge. She also spends time gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables and herbs. Reading religious literature, biographies of notable women, and listening to storytelling sessions are among her favorite pastimes. Socially, she hosts tea‑and‑chat gatherings for neighbours, loves to engage in lively discussions, and occasionally volunteers to organize cultural festivals.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Attending art exhibitions", "Folk dance performances", "Church music concerts", "Experimenting with fusion cooking", "Participating in community quiz nights", "Playing bingo", "Gardening (vegetables & herbs)", "Reading religious literature", "Reading biographies of notable women", "Listening to storytelling sessions", "Hosting tea‑and‑chat gatherings", "Organising cultural festivals", "Engaging in lively discussions"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Ellen aspires to turn her lifelong experience of household management and community service into a more formal role as a cultural ambassador and mentor in Dimapur. She hopes to organize regular workshops that teach traditional Naga and Manipuri crafts, cooking and storytelling to younger generations, and to lead literacy clubs for women who missed formal schooling. She also aims to volunteer as a chaplain‑assistant in her church, supporting elders and coordinating charitable drives. Over the next few years, she wishes to document her family's oral histories in both Manipuri and Assamese, creating a small community archive. Ultimately, she wants to be recognised as a reliable pillar who bridges generations and cultures while staying emotionally resilient.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 52-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with primary education, residing in urban Dimapur, Nagaland. Her first language is Manipuri, and she is fluent in Assamese. She is a community pillar who facilitates cultural events and church activities, applying multilingual skills and meticulous planning. Her lifestyle includes gardening, morning walks, and participation in local volleyball.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Nagaland, district=Dimapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 52
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Nagaland
- district: Dimapur
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 52-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with primary education, residing in urban Dimapur, Nagaland. Her first language is Manipuri, and she is fluent in Assamese. She is a community pillar who facilitates cultural events and church activities, applying multilingual skills and meticulous planning. Her lifestyle includes gardening, morning walks, and participation in local volleyball.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]

- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

4. कॉग्निटिव बिहेवियरल थेरेपीक लेल काउंसलिंग साइकोलॉजिस्टक पास रेफरल
5. 2 सप्ताहमे फॉलो-अप अपॉइंटमेंट निर्धारित कयल गेल अछि

मरीजकेँ नियमित निद्राक समय बना कऽ रखबाक, हल्का शारीरिक गतिविधिमे शामिल होयबाक, आ तनाव प्रबंधन तकनीकक अभ्यास करबाक सलाह देल गेल। परिवारक सदस्यसभकेँ बीमारी आ उपचारक अनुपालनक बारेमे परामर्श देल गेल।

कुल अनुमानित लागत: दवाई आ परामर्श शुल्कक लेल Rs. 15,000. पॉलिसीक शर्तक अनुसार बीमा कवरेज लागू अछि।

TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Geeta Devi]] [[AGE|55]] [[GENDER|Female]] [[MRN|MRN-MDHMC-2024-001]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|203835321155]]
Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Madhubani District Mental Health Center]] District [[DISTRICT|Madhubani]]
Motor / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|BR01AB1234]] (exactly once)
PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|FGHIJ5678K]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0005678]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|603000123456789]]
Bank routing number [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|021000021]]
Credit card [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|5111111111111111]] CVV [[CVV|456]] PIN [[PIN|7890]]
Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431234567]]

Patient Geeta Devi, a 55-year-old female bee-keeper from Madhubani district, presented with chief complaint of persistent low mood, anhedonia, and sleep disturbances for the past 3 months. She reports decreased appetite, fatigue, and feelings of worthlessness. The patient has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

On examination, she appears depressed with psychomotor retardation, reduced speech output, and poor eye contact. Her vitals are stable: BP 140/90 mmHg, pulse 78/min, temperature 98.4°F. Laboratory investigations show HbA1c of 8.2% and fasting blood glucose of 165 mg/dL.

Diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, Severe, with anxious distress
Secondary: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension

Treatment plan:
1. Started on Sertraline 50mg daily, titrated to 100mg over 2 weeks
2. Metformin 500mg twice daily for diabetes control
3. Amlodipine 5mg daily for blood pressure management
4. Referral to counseling psychologist for cognitive behavioral therapy
5. Follow-up appointment scheduled in 2 weeks

The patient was advised to maintain regular sleep schedule, engage in light physical activity, and practice stress management techniques. Family members were counseled about the condition and treatment adherence.

Total estimated cost: Rs. 15,000 for medication and consultation fees. Insurance coverage applicable as per policy terms.

payload.arts_persona: A cultural aficionado, Gopal Baladar frequently attends Yakshagana shows by the Chandramukhi troupe, practices mridangam to the rhythm of M. Balamuralikrishna’s Carnatic renditions, sketches fashion concepts while being inspired by the photography of Prasad K. V., and enjoys the folk beats of Dollu Kunitha during village festivals.
payload.travel_persona: A budget‑conscious explorer, Gopal Baladar enjoys weekend trips to Bengaluru for design expos, yearly pilgrimages to Mysore during Dasara, occasional escapes to the serene backwaters of Kerala, and dreams of a future fashion‑inspired pilgrimage to Paris, always planning trips around festival calendars and affordable train fares.
payload.culinary_persona: A home‑cooking enthusiast, Gopal Baladar loves whipping up bisi bele bath with freshly ground sesame oil and tamarind pulp, perfects ragi mudde for a hearty breakfast, and often savors a steaming plate of masala dosa with coconut chutney, while he occasionally experiments with fusion toppings like cheese and jalapeño.
payload.persona: A curious, organized 23‑year‑old fashion designer, Gopal Baladar blends rural handloom heritage with modern sustainability, while juggling a love for mridangam beats, weekend treks, and an occasional caffeine‑driven late‑night sketch session.
payload.cultural_background: Gopal grows up in the rural hinterland of Hassan district in Karnataka. His family belongs to the Kannada-speaking agrarian community, steeped in local festivals like Ugadi, Sankranti, and the vibrant folk traditions of Yakshagana and Dollu Kunitha. The proximity to neighboring Andhra Pradesh brings a Telugu cultural influence in family ties and cuisine. The region’s strong Hindu ethos, with daily visits to the local Shiva temple and participation in village fairs, shapes his worldview. The modest rural setting instills a sense of practical resourcefulness (jugaad) while exposing him to the rich handloom heritage of the state.
payload.linguistic_background: Gopal's primary language is Kannada, which he speaks fluently and reads and writes with native proficiency. He learned Telugu at home from relatives and is comfortable conversing and reading Telugu script, though his writing is modest. His technical diploma involved instruction in English, giving him functional proficiency for reading manuals, drafting pattern notes, and communicating with suppliers; he can hold everyday conversations in English but may mix in Kannada. Hindi exposure is limited to school basics; he can understand simple spoken Hindi and read Devanagari signs, but he is not confident writing in Hindi.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Gopal's core expertise lies in the fashion and garment sector. He is adept at pattern making, fabric selection, and garment fitting, ensuring that dresses meet both aesthetic and functional standards. He conducts quality inspections and trend analysis to keep collections relevant. His technical diploma equipped him with basic CAD (Computer-Aided Design) for fashion, enabling him to draft simple design sketches digitally. Beyond technical skills, Gopal exhibits strong organizational planning, inventory management, and reliable customer service, often coordinating orders and deliveries for local boutiques. Multilingual communication in Kannada and Telugu enhances his ability to liaise with regional suppliers.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Pattern Making','Fabric Selection','Garment Fitting','Quality Inspection','Trend Analysis','Basic CAD for Fashion','Organizational Planning','Inventory Management','Customer Service','Multilingual Communication (Kannada, Telugu)']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Gopal enjoys exploring Karnataka’s folk arts such as Yakshagana and Dollu Kunitha, often attending local performances. He listens to Carnatic music and practices playing the mridangam on weekends. Sketching fashion ideas and experimenting with fabric draping are his creative outlets. Photography, especially capturing rural landscapes and textile details, keeps him engaged. He loves trekking in the Kabini hills, cooking traditional dishes like bisi bele bath, and volunteering at village festivals to help organize cultural programs.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Yakshagana Performances','Dollu Kunitha Dance','Listening to Carnatic Music','Playing Mridangam','Fashion Sketching','Fabric Draping Experiments','Photography (Rural Landscapes, Textile Details)','Trekking in Kabini Hills','Cooking Traditional Dishes (Bisi Bele Bath)','Volunteering at Village Festivals']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Gopal aims to evolve from a dress diver into a senior fashion designer specializing in sustainable, regionally-inspired garments. Over the next five years, he plans to enroll in a diploma in fashion design, master advanced CAD tools, and acquire certification in eco‑friendly textile practices. He aspires to launch his own boutique in Hassan that showcases handloom fabrics from Karnataka and blends them with contemporary designs, thereby creating employment opportunities for local artisans. Ultimately, he envisions his brand becoming a recognized name for ethical fashion across South India.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Hassan.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 23
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Dress Diver
- education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka

- clinical_persona_summary: A 23‑year‑old male from rural Hassan, Karnataka, works as a dress diver with a technical diploma, is never married, and speaks Kannada as his primary language. He is organized and maintains a sustainable fashion design practice that blends local handloom heritage with modern aesthetics. His lifestyle includes weekend treks, occasional caffeine use, and a balanced diet of traditional dishes like bisi bele bath and ragi mudde.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]

Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]

- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಯ ಸಂಶೋಧನೆಗಳು: ಎಡಗೈ ಹಿಂಭಾಗದಲ್ಲಿ ವ್ಯಾಪಕವಾದ ಊತ ಮತ್ತು ನೀಲಿಗಟ್ಟುವಿಕೆ ಕಂಡುಬಂದಿದೆ. ಮೆಟಾಕಾರ್ಪೊಫ್ಯಾಲಂಜಿಯಲ್ ಕೀಲುಗಳ ಮೇಲೆ ಮೃದುತ್ವ ಕಂಡುಬಂದಿದೆ. ನೋವಿನಿಂದಾಗಿ ಸಕ್ರಿಯ ಚಲನೆಯ ವ್ಯಾಪ್ತಿಯು ಸೀಮಿತವಾಗಿದೆ. ತೆರೆದ ಗಾಯಗಳು ಅಥವಾ ಸೀಳುಗಳಿಲ್ಲ. ಕ್ಯಾಪಿಲರಿ ರಿಫಿಲ್ ಸಾಮಾನ್ಯವಾಗಿದೆ. ಎಲ್ಲಾ ಬೆರಳುಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಸಂವೇದನೆ ಸರಿಯಾಗಿದೆ.

ರೋಗನಿರ್ಣಯ: ಎಡಗೈ ಮೃದು ಅಂಗಾಂಶದ ಗಾಯ ಮತ್ತು ಉಳುಕು.

ಸೂಚನೆಗಳು: 1 ವಾರಕ್ಕೆ ಕಠಿಣ ಕೆಲಸವನ್ನು ತಪ್ಪಿಸಿ. ಪ್ರತಿ 2 ಗಂಟೆಗೊಮ್ಮೆ 15 ನಿಮಿಷಗಳ ಕಾಲ ಐಸ್ ಪ್ಯಾಕ್‌ಗಳನ್ನು ಹಚ್ಚಿ. ಸೂಚಿಸಿದ ಔಷಧಿಗಳನ್ನು ನಿರ್ದೇಶಿಸಿದಂತೆ ತೆಗೆದುಕೊಳ್ಳಿ.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Hassan]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-HSN-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ganesh Kulkarni]] | DOB [[DOB|2001-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|23]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Dress Diver]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Shankar Kumar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Hassan]]

Chief complaint: Pain and swelling over left hand with inability to grip after work-related injury

History of present illness: Patient is a 23-year-old male dress diver who presents with acute onset of severe pain and swelling over the dorsum of left hand after accidentally catching his hand in a sewing machine while working on traditional handloom fabric. Pain started immediately with visible swelling and difficulty moving fingers. No history of loss of consciousness.

Examination findings: Left hand shows diffuse swelling over dorsum with ecchymosis. Tenderness noted over metacarpophalangeal joints. Active range of motion limited due to pain. No open wounds or lacerations. Capillary refill normal. Sensation intact in all digits.

Investigations: X-ray left hand shows no fractures. Soft tissue swelling noted.

Diagnosis: Soft tissue contusion and sprain of left hand

Treatment plan: Prescribed analgesics, anti-inflammatory medication, and advised rest with elevation. Follow-up in 3 days. Referral to hand specialist if no improvement.

Instructions: Avoid heavy work for 1 week. Apply ice packs for 15 minutes every 2 hours. Take prescribed medications as directed.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thrissur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 68
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Labourer, Other
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Urban
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 68-year-old female labourer from Thrissur, Kerala, who is currently married and speaks Malayalam. She has a literate but non-formal education level and lives in an urban zone. Her lifestyle includes traditional crafts, community involvement, and frugal habits.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:

GOOD:

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Pulwama.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 20
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Pulwama
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 20‑year‑old male from Pulwama, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married, has no formal occupation, and is illiterate, with a rural residence. His first language is Kashmiri, and he is fluent in Urdu and English. He lives in a close‑knit Kashmiri Muslim community and follows traditional customs.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Please bring your MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-1120-001]] and arrive 15 mins early. Contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419234567]] for queries.

payload.professional_persona: A seasoned poultry farm manager, Khandu Gavde blends meticulous operational planning with hands‑on animal health expertise, they lead their team with methodical precision and a friendly, talkative demeanor that makes them the go‑to problem‑solver in the village agribusiness community.
payload.linguistic_persona: A 50‑year‑old native Marathi speaker, Khandu Gavde is illiterate but can recognize signs and numbers, they understand basic Hindi from market conversations while not speaking English.
payload.sports_persona: An avid cricket enthusiast, Khandu Gavde regularly organizes friendly matches for the local village team, they cheer loudly from the sidelines, and they also follow the Mumbai Indians in the IPL, mixing grassroots play with national fandom.
payload.arts_persona: A folk music aficionado, Khandu Gavde enjoys Marathi bhajans and lavani by Shahir Sable and classical renditions of Bhimsen Joshi, they often sing along while tending the poultry houses and attend village melas to watch traditional dance performances.
payload.travel_persona: A travel enthusiast, Khandu Gavde loves short trips to nearby hill stations like Mahabaleshwar and coastal towns such as Ratnagiri, they prefer traveling by bus with family and enjoy exploring local temples and markets while taking ample rest.
payload.culinary_persona: A connoisseur of traditional regional cuisine, Khandu Gavde prefers bhakri with varan, pithla, aamti, and tangy raw mango chutney, they relish fresh eggs from their farm but seldom cook themselves, relying on family members for most meals.
payload.persona: Khandu Gavde is a methodical, sociable poultry farm manager who mixes disciplined planning with lively village interactions, yet occasionally skips medication doses and relies on gold savings.
payload.cultural_background: He belongs to the Marathi agrarian community of Kolhapur in rural Maharashtra. Growing up in a farming environment, his cultural outlook is shaped by traditional Marathi customs, communal festivals, and a deep respect for land and livestock. The local culture emphasizes hospitality, collective celebrations, and the practical values of self‑reliance, which align with his preference for structure and predictability.
payload.linguistic_background: Khandu speaks Marathi as his mother tongue and uses it for all daily communication. Being illiterate, he cannot read or write Marathi or any other language, though he can recognize simple signs and numbers. He has basic listening comprehension of Hindi due to exposure in the market, but cannot converse fluently. He does not speak English.
payload.skills_and_expertise: With decades of hands‑on experience managing a poultry farm, Khandu has developed expertise in operational planning, animal health management, feed formulation, staff supervision, and cost control. He is adept at troubleshooting farm‑related issues, coordinating market sales, and maintaining relationships with suppliers and local customers. His strong interpersonal skills enable him to lead a team effectively and resolve conflicts quickly. He also possesses practical knowledge of basic agribusiness finance through mental calculations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Poultry farm management", "Animal health monitoring", "Feed formulation", "Staff supervision", "Cost control and budgeting", "Market sales coordination", "Supplier relationship management", "Team leadership", "Conflict resolution", "Basic agribusiness finance (mental arithmetic)"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Khandu enjoys participating in village melas and religious festivals, where he can socialize and share stories. He is fond of listening to Marathi folk music and bhajans, often singing along while working in the farmyard. Cricket matches in the local field provide recreation, and he occasionally organizes friendly games. He also likes growing a small kitchen garden of seasonal vegetables, and he spends evenings reading (or listening to) informational pamphlets about poultry health and innovative farming methods.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Attending village melas and religious festivals", "Listening to and singing Marathi folk music and bhajans", "Playing cricket in the local field", "Maintaining a small kitchen garden", "Reading or listening to pamphlets on poultry health and innovative farming methods"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: As a 50‑year‑old manager, Khandu aims to further increase the productivity of his poultry farm by introducing better disease‑prevention protocols and modern feed technologies. He plans to expand the farm’s capacity, possibly by acquiring adjacent land, and to diversify into a small feed‑mill to supply nearby growers. He also aspires to mentor younger farmers in the village, sharing his practical knowledge to uplift the local agrarian community. Securing financial stability for his family and leaving a well‑managed, reputable farm for his children is a central ambition.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Khandu Gavde, a 50-year-old male manager for a poultry farm in rural Kolhapur, Maharashtra, is currently married and has no formal education. He speaks Marathi as his first language and is illiterate, relying on basic listening comprehension. His lifestyle includes occasional non-adherence to medication and a preference for saving gold over seeking formal financial advice.

  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Kolhapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 50
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Manager, Poultry Farm
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Kolhapur
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Khandu Gavde, a 50-year-old male manager for a poultry farm in rural Kolhapur, Maharashtra, is currently married and has no formal education. He speaks Marathi as his first language and is illiterate, relying on basic listening comprehension. His lifestyle includes occasional non-adherence to medication and a preference for saving gold over seeking formal financial advice.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
