Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Chittoor.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18

- occupation: Petrol Pump Salesman

He is never married and balances his practical curiosity with over‑worrying, often double‑checking safety procedures. He follows cricket and occasionally jogs, and his functional English enables customer interactions.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; wrap facility/geo as tags — never plain-only):

Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).
TYPE aliases forbidden: ADDRESS_NUMBER→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS;
RECEIPT_NUMBER→plain; STATE→plain or DISTRICT.

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]

Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kishanganj PHC]] Village [[VILLAGE|Mahipur]] District [[DISTRICT|Kishanganj]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Rahim Ansari]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Surgical OPD
Chief Complaint: Patient presents with a deep laceration on the left forearm with a retained foreign body.
History of Present Illness: The patient, a painting supervisor, reports that while at a worksite in Mahipur, a sharp metal sheet fell and scraped his left forearm. He immediately applied pressure with a cloth but bleeding persisted. He was brought to the PHC by his wife.
Examination Findings: On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Vital signs are stable. A 5x2 cm linear laceration is present on the volar aspect of the left forearm. A small, hard, non-reactive object is palpable at the base of the wound. There is no significant swelling or erythema. Distal neurovascular status is intact.
Investigations: X-ray of the left forearm was taken, which confirms a radiopaque foreign body. No bony injury is noted.
Diagnosis: Lacerated wound with retained foreign body, left forearm.
Treatment: Under aseptic conditions in the minor OT, the wound was cleaned with normal saline. The foreign body was successfully removed with a pair of forceps. The wound was irrigated with povidone-iodine solution and dressed with an antiseptic ointment and a sterile gauze dressing. Tetanus toxoid was administered as per protocol. The patient was given a course of oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500mg) and analgesics (Paracetamol 500mg).
Plan: Patient advised to return in 48 hours for wound dressing review. He was counseled on wound care and signs of infection. Follow-up scheduled. The ASHA worker, [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Fatima Begum]], will be informed of the follow-up plan.

payload.cultural_background: Ahmed comes from an urban Muslim family in Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, where a deep appreciation for classical Indian culture coexists with Islamic traditions. His household places a strong emphasis on learning Sanskrit as a link to the subcontinent’s literary heritage, while also observing the rituals and community life of the Muslim ummah.
payload.linguistic_background: In terms of language, Ahmed’s mother tongue is Sanskrit, which he reads, writes and speaks fluently thanks to his family’s scholarly tradition and his own schooling up to the 10th standard. He has functional proficiency in English, enough to understand textbooks and communicate in urban settings, while his Hindi skill remains basic, limited to everyday interactions and informal listening.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Ahmed’s expertise lies primarily in classical language studies and related cultural skills. He is proficient in reading, interpreting, and reciting Sanskrit texts, including Vedic hymns and epics. His calligraphic skills enable him to write Devanagari script with precision. He also possesses basic digital literacy, such as using a smartphone and internet for educational purposes, and is adept at modest home management tasks. His moderate interpersonal communication reflects his balanced social nature, and his emotional reactivity drives him to practice yoga and meditation for self‑regulation.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Sanskrit reading', 'Sanskrit recitation', 'Devanagari calligraphy', 'Interpretation of Vedic texts', 'Basic digital literacy', 'Smartphone usage for learning', 'Home management tasks', 'Moderate interpersonal communication']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Ahmed enjoys reading Sanskrit epics and philosophical texts, listening to Qawwali and Sufi music, practicing yoga and meditation for emotional balance, sketching calligraphic art, playing cricket informally with friends in the local park, exploring street food in Kanpur, and experimenting with simple cooking recipes at home. payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Ahmed aspires to pursue higher education in Sanskrit literature, aiming for a Bachelor’s degree followed by a Master’s to become a teacher or researcher in a university or cultural institution. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently unemployed and never married. His first language is Sanskrit, and he resides in an urban zone. Clinically, he reports anxiety‑driven yoga and frequent missed deadlines, alongside a preference for street‑food consumption.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]], District [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Rohan Sharma]], [[AGE|19]] / [[GENDER|Male]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
परीक्षण-निष्कर्षः: रोगिणः निरन्तर-श्रान्तिः अकारण-भार-क्षयः च पूर्व-त्रिसप्ताह-काले उपस्थिताः सन्ति। वर्धिता चिन्ता अध्ययने एकाग्रता-कठिनता च सूचिता। परिवार-इतिहासः पूर्व-प्रारम्भिक-अर्बुद-रोगस्य कृते महत्त्वपूर्णः अस्ति। कानपुर-मण्डल-चिकित्सालयं प्रति व्यापक-अर्बुद-रोग-परीक्षणाय रक्त-परीक्षणाय च तत्काल-प्रेषणस्य अनुशंसा कृता, यत्र पूर्ण-रक्त-गणना चयापचय-पटलः च सम्मिलितौ स्तः।

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Vikram Sharma]] रोगिणः स्थितेः विषये सूचितः। [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] सत्यापितः। [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] पुष्टः। [[RELIGION|Hindu]] अभिलेखेषु अङ्कितः।
payload.generated_text_en:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]], District [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Rohan Sharma]], [[AGE|19]] / [[GENDER|Male]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Visit findings: Patient presents with persistent fatigue and unexplained weight loss over past 3 weeks. Reports increased anxiety and difficulty concentrating on studies. Family history significant for early-onset cancers. Recommended immediate referral to District Hospital Kanpur for comprehensive oncology evaluation and blood work including complete blood count and metabolic panel.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Vikram Sharma]] contacted regarding patient's condition. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] verified. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] confirmed. [[RELIGION|Hindu]] noted in records.

payload.arts_persona: Ejaz Alam frequents local mehfil gatherings where Urdu poetry is recited, loves listening to qawwalis by the Sabri Brothers, and unwinds with Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan, letting artistic expression fuel their creative spontaneity.
payload.travel_persona: Ejaz Alam prefers short road trips to nearby towns such as Darbhanga and Patna, dreams of a pilgrimage to the historic city of Varanasi, and plans travel around family commitments and modest savings, favoring affordable train journeys.
payload.culinary_persona: Ejaz Alam relishes Bihari staples like litti chokha, sattu paratha, and mustard‑seed fish curry, enjoys adding a pinch of cumin to the family biryani during festive meals, and helps his wife in the kitchen with moderate cooking skill.
payload.persona: Ejaz Alam is a hands‑on painter who thrives on Urdu poetry, balances spontaneous tool splurges with modest savings, and habitually checks their blood pressure on a whim; they see each brushstroke as a meditation.
payload.cultural_background: Ejaz Alam belongs to a close‑knit Urdu‑speaking Muslim community in the rural district of Kishanganj, Bihar. His upbringing is shaped by the agrarian lifestyle of the region, interwoven with the literary and cultural traditions of Urdu—such as mushairas, folk songs, and the observance of Islamic festivals like Eid. Community ties, shared meals, and local celebrations play a central role in his daily life.
payload.linguistic_background: Ejaz’s mother tongue is Urdu, which he speaks fluently and uses for everyday conversation, cultural expression, and religious practices. He can read and write basic Urdu script, though his limited formal schooling restricts his literacy to simple texts such as notices and labels. Hindi is his second language; he communicates fluently in spoken Hindi and understands local dialects, but his reading and writing abilities in Devanagari are rudimentary. He does not possess a third language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Ejaz is skilled in surface preparation, priming, and applying interior and exterior paints on residential and small commercial structures. He has strong experience in plastering walls and ceilings, erecting and dismantling scaffolding, and using hand tools safely. He can interpret basic construction drawings to plan work sequences, procure required materials, and supervise a crew of laborers, ensuring quality and safety standards are met. He also communicates effectively with clients and suppliers in both Urdu and Hindi.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['surface preparation', 'interior and exterior painting', 'plastering', 'scaffolding', 'team supervision', 'material procurement', 'basic construction drawing interpretation', 'safety compliance', 'client communication']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Ejaz likes to play cricket with neighbours, attend local mehfil gatherings where Urdu poetry is recited, listen to devotional qawwalis, and enjoy cooking traditional Bihari dishes with his wife. He also enjoys fixing small household repairs, watching Bollywood movies, and spending evenings chatting with his extended family.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['cricket', 'attending Urdu poetry gatherings (mehfil)', 'listening to qawwali', 'cooking Bihari dishes', 'home repairs', 'watching Bollywood movies', 'socializing with extended family']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Ejaz aims to transition from supervisory roles to becoming an independent contractor who can manage larger construction projects. He plans to acquire formal certification in surface coating technologies, learn modern painting techniques, and eventually set up his own painting and refurbishment firm serving the surrounding villages. He also hopes to improve his financial stability to provide better education for his children.

payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 28-year-old male from rural Kishanganj, Bihar, currently married, with a Below Primary education level. payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Kishanganj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Supervisors and Foremen and Related Trades Workers in Painting, Building Structure
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Kishanganj
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 28-year-old male from rural Kishanganj, Bihar, currently married, with a Below Primary education level. He works as a painting supervisor and foreman, speaks Urdu as his first language, and has limited formal literacy. Clinically, he reports occasional blood pressure checks and a lifestyle involving modest savings and spontaneous tool purchases. His community is a close-knit Urdu-speaking Muslim group in a rural agrarian setting.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.arts_persona: Sakshi Narvekar passionately paints bright coastal vistas inspired by Goan artist Laxmikant Shet, listens to Konkani folk singer Anjali Nair, dances Dhalo to the rhythms of local drum troupes, and crafts intricate traditional embroidery reminiscent of coastal motifs, and they find creative joy in these pursuits.
payload.travel_persona: Sakshi Narvekar prefers short road trips to nearby beaches like Palolem, weekend getaways to the historic forts of Old Goa, and dreams of a modest pilgrimage to Varanasi, and they plan loosely while relying on WhatsApp groups for tips, balancing family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Sakshi Narvekar delights in preparing authentic Goan fish curry with coconut milk and kokum, steaming fluffy sannas, cooking pork vindaloo, and experimenting with herbal chutneys of coriander, mint, and tamarind, while they also enjoy sol kadhi and sweet bebinca at family gatherings.
payload.persona: Sakshi Narvekar is a spontaneous 24‑year‑old home chef and artist who blends vibrant coastal cooking with lively folk crafts, thrives on community festivals, and occasionally procrastinates on paperwork while they juggle family and entrepreneurial dreams.
payload.cultural_background: Sakshi belongs to the Konkani-speaking community of South Goa. Growing up in a rural village, she has been immersed in the vibrant traditions of Goan Hindu culture, including local festivals like Shigmo and the community’s devotion to Shiva and Vishnu deities. The agrarian lifestyle and close-knit family ties shape her worldview, while the mix of Konkani, Marathi, and Portuguese heritage adds a touch of coastal cosmopolitan flavor.
payload.linguistic_background: She speaks Konkani as her mother tongue and can read and write it at a basic level thanks to primary schooling. She is fluent in Marathi, comfortable in everyday conversation and familiar with Marathi script. Her English is limited to simple spoken communication, mainly through television, WhatsApp, and local interactions. Her literacy is functional, allowing her to manage household paperwork and understand basic instructions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sakshi excels at preparing authentic Goan dishes, especially fish curry and sannas, and can organize family meals for festivals. She efficiently manages household finances and budgeting, oversees child care for her young children, and coordinates community gatherings during local festivals. She possesses basic digital skills, including using WhatsApp, smartphone camera, and social media to stay connected. Additionally, she crafts traditional Konkani embroidery and beadwork for personal and gifting purposes.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Goan home cooking', 'Household budgeting', 'Child care', 'Community event coordination', 'Basic digital communication (WhatsApp, social media)', 'Traditional Konkani embroidery and beadwork']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys painting vibrant coastal scenes, listening to Konkani folk music, dancing to traditional Dhalo and Ghumat beats, gardening herbs used in cooking, exploring new recipes, attending local cultural festivals, and chatting with friends over tea at community gatherings.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Painting coastal scenes', 'Listening to Konkani folk music', 'Traditional dance (Dhalo, Ghumat)', 'Herb gardening', 'Experimenting with new recipes', 'Attending local festivals', 'Socializing over tea with friends']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sakshi aims to transform her culinary passion into a small home‑based catering venture that offers authentic Goan delicacies at local events and festivals. She also wishes to enhance her skills in arts and crafts, perhaps by taking community workshops, and hopes to become a mentor for other homemakers in her village. Though she has only primary education, she plans to attend adult literacy programs to improve her reading and writing, enabling her to handle business paperwork more confidently. Ultimately, she envisions a balanced life where she can support her family, nurture her children, and contribute to the cultural vibrancy of her community.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 24-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker with primary education, residing in rural South Goa. She is Konkani-speaking, fluent in Marathi, and follows Goan Hindu cultural traditions. Clinically, she has a diet rich in coastal fish and coconut, with limited structured exercise.

payload.travel_persona: Tinath Das prefers short trips to nearby pilgrimage sites like Puri and the Sun Temple at Konark, planning visits around festival seasons, while dreaming of a future trek to the Himalayas but constrained by farm responsibilities and modest savings, and they balance adventure with family considerations.

payload.persona: Tinath Das balances a structured sericulture business with laid‑back habits, community spirit, and a modest quest for literacy, all while they keep their health, finances, and love for folk culture in check.
payload.cultural_background: Tinath lives in the rural heartland of Dhenkanal, Odisha, where the Odia community’s cultural fabric is woven around agriculture and sericulture. Village life follows a structured calendar of festivals such as Raja, Durga Puja and Pana Sankranti, which prescribe specific rituals and communal gatherings. At the same time, daily routines are informal and adaptable, allowing Tinath to balance his responsibilities with spontaneous social interactions. Respect for elders, shared labor during the mulberry and silkworm cycles, and a strong sense of community identity define his cultural backdrop.
payload.linguistic_background: Tinath’s mother tongue is Odia, which he speaks fluently in every aspect of his daily life—from chatting with neighbours in the panchayat to negotiating cocoon prices at the local market. Over the years, he has picked up functional spoken English through interactions with agricultural extension officers and cooperative members, enabling him to understand basic instructions and conduct simple transactions. However, because he is illiterate, he cannot read or write in Odia or English; his literacy is limited to recognising common symbols, numbers, and a few brand logos.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Tinath has practical expertise in sericulture, including mulberry cultivation, silkworm rearing, disease management, and cocoon harvesting. He manages a small‑scale farm, coordinating the timing of leaf supply and temperature control to optimise silkworm growth. His hands‑on experience extends to basic market negotiation for cocoons, maintaining cooperative relationships with local buyers, and training younger villagers in traditional rearing techniques. Additionally, he possesses useful knowledge of village‑level farm administration and collective decision‑making.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Silkworm rearing', 'Mulberry cultivation', 'Cocoon harvesting', 'Farm management', 'Market negotiation', 'Cooperative leadership']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Tinath enjoys participating in village cultural events, especially folk singing and traditional dance during festivals. He is an avid fan of local cricket matches, often gathering with friends to watch games on a communal television. In his leisure time, he likes tending to a small kitchen garden, growing vegetables like gourds and leafy greens, and playing cards with neighbours during evenings. He also spends time listening to devotional bhajans on the radio.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Traditional dance', 'Watching cricket matches', 'Kitchen gardening', 'Playing cards', 'Listening to bhajans']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Tinath aims to double his cocoon production over the next five years by adopting disease‑resistant silkworm breeds and improving mulberry yield through better irrigation techniques. He aspires to form a village sericulture cooperative that can secure fair prices from buyers, provide shared resources for training, and establish a direct link to regional silk processing units. Additionally, he wishes to acquire basic literacy skills through adult education programs to better manage records and communicate with market stakeholders.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Dhenkanal]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-DK-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Gopal Pradhan]] | DOB [[DOB|1976-05-12]] | Age: [[AGE|48]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Rearer, Silkworm]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Mishra]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sushila Pradhan]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438912345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-DK-042]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Dhenkanal]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with fever and weight loss
History of present illness: Patient reports productive cough with yellowish sputum, low-grade fever in evenings, and progressive weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the past month. He also complains of occasional chest discomfort and night sweats.
Past medical history: Diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus 2 years ago, on oral hypoglycemic agents. No known history of TB or other chronic illnesses.
Physical examination: Temperature 100.8°F, pulse 92/min, respiratory rate 18/min, BP 130/85 mmHg. General condition: moderately ill, cachectic. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in right upper zone with occasional rhonchi.
Investigations: Sputum for AFB smear positive (2+), GeneXpert MTB/RIF positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, HbA1c 8.2%, random blood sugar 180 mg/dL.
Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Drug-sensitive strain, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Treatment plan:
1. Anti-TB treatment: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol for 2 months (intensive phase), followed by Isoniazid and Rifampicin for 4 months (continuation phase)
2. Diabetes management: Metformin 500mg twice daily, monitor blood glucose levels
3. Nutritional support: High-protein diet, vitamin supplements
4. Follow-up: Review in 2 weeks for treatment response and side effects
5. DOTS: Daily observed therapy at nearest health center
6. Counseling: Emphasize treatment adherence, importance of completing full course, and diabetes management
Advice: Rest adequately, maintain hydration, report any adverse drug reactions immediately. Return for follow-up on 2024-08-29.
payload.judge_reasoning: Odia prose matches expected script/language; all entity TYPEs in allow-list; persona (male 48, Dhenkanal) and TB+DM domain fit; no mismatches, invented types, or length issues.

payload.professional_persona: Tinath Das, a 48‑year‑old sericulture practitioner, runs a small mulberry farm and silkworm rearing operation, applying systematic leaf‑supply schedules while informally negotiating cocoon prices and mentoring village youths, balancing their love for structure with a relaxed attitude toward paperwork.
payload.linguistic_persona: Tinath Das communicates fluently in Odia for everyday interactions, speaks basic English in market dealings, yet remains illiterate, relying on symbol recognition for reading, and they often ask neighbours to help with paperwork.
payload.sports_persona: Tinath Das is an avid follower of village cricket matches, cheering for the local state team and gathering with friends around a communal television to watch IPL games, and they occasionally join a local walking club for morning exercise.
payload.arts_persona: Tinath Das enjoys singing Sambalpuri folk songs and performing traditional dances during Raja and Durga Puja, frequently listening to bhajans of Anuradha Paudwal and the melodious tunes of Odia legend Prafulla Kar on their radio, and they cherish these cultural expressions as a vital part of their identity.
payload.travel_persona: Tinath Das prefers short trips to nearby pilgrimage sites like Puri and the Sun Temple at Konark, planning visits around festival seasons, while dreaming of a future trek to the Himalayas but constrained by farm responsibilities and modest savings, and they balance adventure with family considerations.
payload.culinary_persona: Tinath Das favors simple, hearty meals like dalma with pakhala, savoring seasonal vegetables from their garden such as gourds and leafy greens, and they delight in the tangy flavor of mango‑panch phoron chutney, though they rarely cook elaborate dishes.
payload.persona: Tinath Das balances a structured sericulture business with laid‑back habits, community spirit, and a modest quest for literacy, all while they keep their health, finances, and love for folk culture in check.
payload.cultural_background: Tinath lives in the rural heartland of Dhenkanal, Odisha, where the Odia community’s cultural fabric is woven around agriculture and sericulture. Village life follows a structured calendar of festivals such as Raja, Durga Puja and Pana Sankranti, which prescribe specific rituals and communal gatherings. At the same time, daily routines are informal and adaptable, allowing Tinath to balance his responsibilities with spontaneous social interactions. Respect for elders, shared labor during the mulberry and silkworm cycles, and a strong sense of community identity define his cultural backdrop.
payload.linguistic_background: Tinath’s mother tongue is Odia, which he speaks fluently in every aspect of his daily life—from chatting with neighbours in the panchayat to negotiating cocoon prices at the local market. Over the years, he has picked up functional spoken English through interactions with agricultural extension officers and cooperative members, enabling him to understand basic instructions and conduct simple transactions. However, because he is illiterate, he cannot read or write in Odia or English; his literacy is limited to recognising common symbols, numbers, and a few brand logos.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Tinath has practical expertise in sericulture, including mulberry cultivation, silkworm rearing, disease management, and cocoon harvesting. He manages a small‑scale farm, coordinating the timing of leaf supply and temperature control to optimise silkworm growth. His hands‑on experience extends to basic market negotiation for cocoons, maintaining cooperative relationships with local buyers, and training younger villagers in traditional rearing techniques. Additionally, he possesses useful knowledge of village‑level farm administration and collective decision‑making.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Silkworm rearing', 'Mulberry cultivation', 'Cocoon harvesting', 'Farm management', 'Market negotiation', 'Cooperative leadership']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Tinath enjoys participating in village cultural events, especially folk singing and traditional dance during festivals. He is an avid fan of local cricket matches, often gathering with friends to watch games on a communal television. In his leisure time, he likes tending to a small kitchen garden, growing vegetables like gourds and leafy greens, and playing cards with neighbours during evenings. He also spends time listening to devotional bhajans on the radio.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Traditional dance', 'Watching cricket matches', 'Kitchen gardening', 'Playing cards', 'Listening to bhajans']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Tinath aims to double his cocoon production over the next five years by adopting disease‑resistant silkworm breeds and improving mulberry yield through better irrigation techniques. He aspires to form a village sericulture cooperative that can secure fair prices from buyers, provide shared resources for training, and establish a direct link to regional silk processing units. Additionally, he wishes to acquire basic literacy skills through adult education programs to better manage records and communicate with market stakeholders.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 48-year-old male from rural Dhenkanal, Odisha, works in sericulture and is currently married with an illiterate education. His first language is Odia, and he maintains a modest lifestyle with community-oriented habits. He follows a structured routine for his silkworm business while engaging in local cultural activities.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Dhenkanal.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 48
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Rearer, Silkworm
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Dhenkanal
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 48-year-old male from rural Dhenkanal, Odisha, works in sericulture and is currently married with an illiterate education. His first language is Odia, and he maintains a modest lifestyle with community-oriented habits. He follows a structured routine for his silkworm business while engaging in local cultural activities.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ଡାକ୍ତର [[DOCTOR_NAME|ଡାକ୍ତର ପ୍ରଦୀପ]]: ବିସ୍ତୃତ ବିବରଣୀ ପାଇଁ ଧନ୍ୟବାଦ। ଜ୍ୱର, ମୁଣ୍ଡବିନ୍ଧା ଏବଂ ଆଖି ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣା ଲକ୍ଷଣ ଆଧାରରେ, ଆମକୁ ସତର୍କ ରହିବାକୁ ହେବ। ମୁଁ ଆପଣଙ୍କୁ ଡେଙ୍ଗୁ ଏବଂ ମ୍ୟାଲେରିଆ ଯାଞ୍ଚ କରିବା ପାଇଁ ରକ୍ତ ପରୀକ୍ଷା କରିବାକୁ ପରାମର୍ଶ ଦେଉଛି। ଦୟାକରି ଆପଣଙ୍କ ଗ୍ରାମର ନିକଟତମ ପ୍ରାଥମିକ ସ୍ୱାସ୍ଥ୍ୟ କେନ୍ଦ୍ର କିମ୍ବା ଢେଙ୍କାନାଳ ଜିଲ୍ଲା ଡାକ୍ତରଖାନାକୁ ଏକ ସମ୍ପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ରକ୍ତ ଗଣନା (CBC) ଏବଂ ଡେଙ୍ଗୁ/ମ୍ୟାଲେରିଆ ପରୀକ୍ଷା ପାଇଁ ଯାଆନ୍ତୁ। ଏହି ସମୟ ମଧ୍ୟରେ, ଦୟାକରି ପ୍ରଚୁର ପାଣି ପିଅନ୍ତୁ, ଜ୍ୱର ପାଇଁ ପାରାସିଟାମୋଲ୍ 500mg ନିଅନ୍ତୁ ଏବଂ ପର୍ଯ୍ୟାପ୍ତ ବିଶ୍ରାମ କରନ୍ତୁ।

payload.generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Chandra Behera]] [[AGE|48]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-08-15-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438123456]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|ramesh.behera.dhenkanal@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dhenkanal District Hospital]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Doctor, I have been having fever for the last three days. It is coming and going, and my whole body is paining. I feel very weak.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]: Hello Ramesh. I understand you are not feeling well. Can you tell me more? Is the fever continuous or does it spike at specific times? Do you have any headache, body rash, or pain behind the eyes?
Patient: The fever is mostly in the evening, it gets very high then. No rash, but I have a bad headache and my eyes hurt a little. I am from the village, I cannot come to the hospital easily.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]: Thank you for the details. Based on your symptoms of fever with headache and eye pain, we need to be cautious. I recommend you get a blood test done to check for dengue and malaria. Please visit the nearest Primary Health Centre in your village or the Dhenkanal District Hospital for a Complete Blood Count (CBC) and Dengue/Malaria test. In the meantime, please drink plenty of water, take paracetamol 500mg for fever, and get adequate rest.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will go to the PHC tomorrow morning. What should I do if the fever gets worse at night?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]: That is a good plan. If the fever becomes very high (above 103°F), you have severe stomach pain, or you start vomiting, you must come to the Dhenkanal District Hospital immediately or call for an ambulance. Otherwise, continue with the rest and fluids. We will speak again after you get your test reports. Take care.
payload.judge_reasoning: Odia prose throughout clinical narrative; all entity TYPEs on allow-list; Latin IDs/digits/drug/lab terms as expected; male 48yo fever case domain/persona-plausible; no script/dialect or length issues.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
