Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Nepali (code=ne, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Nagaland, district=Kohima.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 48
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Bill and Accounts Collector
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Nagaland
- district: Kohima
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: Mary Lotha, a 48-year-old female, is married and works as a bill and accounts collector in an urban Nagaland setting. She is Nepali-speaking with a middle education level and lives in Kohima district. Her lifestyle includes solitary morning walks and a habit of spontaneous art purchases.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

आशा कार्यकर्ताको टिपोट — गाउँ [[VILLAGE|Jalukie]], जिल्ला [[DISTRICT|Kohima]]
लाभार्थी [[PATIENT_NAME|Mary Lotha]], [[AGE|48]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
आशा कार्यकर्ता: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Imli Angami]] | फोन [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
भ्रमणको निष्कर्ष: बिरामीमा 3 दिनदेखि निरन्तर टाउको दुखाइ र हल्का ज्वरो देखिएको छ। बिरामीले बिहानको पैदल यात्राको समयमा बेलाबेला चक्कर लागेको बताएका छन्। तौलमा कुनै खास परिवर्तन नदेखिएको।

क्लिनिकल मूल्याङ्कन: रक्तचाप 140/90 mmHg, तापक्रम 99.2°F। बिरामीलाई हल्का थकान भएको देखिएको छ। विश्राम र पानी पिउने सल्लाह दिइएको छ। ज्वरोका लागि प्यारासिटामोल सिफारिस गरिएको छ। 3 दिनमा फलो-अप गर्न सिफारिस गरिएको छ।

Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Mary Lotha]], [[AGE|48]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Imli Angami]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient presented with persistent headache and mild fever for 3 days. Reports occasional dizziness during morning walks. No significant weight changes noted.

payload.judge_reasoning: Nepali Devanagari prose correct; all TYPEs in allow-list; persona/clinical fit and surrogate plausibility good; no script or domain mismatches.

payload.professional_persona: Bansidhar Ekka is an aspiring agricultural extension assistant who leverages their hands-on farming experience, bamboo-craft expertise and methodical planning to help village households adopt organic pest-control and water-saving irrigation, while they coordinate community training sessions with reliable punctuality.
payload.linguistic_persona: Bansidhar Ekka speaks their mother tongue fluently, can read and write simple signs in the community’s indigenous script, understands bits of the neighboring state language through daily conversations, but has no formal literacy beyond primary-level basics, and they often rely on visual cues.
payload.sports_persona: Bansidhar Ekka is a regular member of the Sonapur village cricket team and the district’s junior kabaddi squad, training with friends after school, favouring fast-bowling drills and aggressive defensive moves, though they sometimes neglect proper warm-ups due to eagerness to play, and they often avoid injuries by learning from past mistakes.
payload.arts_persona: Bansidhar Ekka enjoys performing the lively Jhumar folk dance during village festivals, playing the tamak with rhythmic precision, and crafting bamboo baskets inspired by local motifs, often listening to folk singer Mohan Bharat and admiring actress Madhubala’s classic movies, and they feel these artistic pursuits keep their spirit vibrant.
payload.travel_persona: Bansidhar Ekka enjoys weekend trips to Baripada for market supplies, occasional pilgrimages to Simlipal National Park to explore forest trails, and dreams of reaching Kolkata’s cultural festivals, balancing modest family funds with the desire for new experiences, and they plan each journey carefully.
payload.culinary_persona: Bansidhar Ekka prefers hearty pakhala with roasted mango pickle, enjoys the tangy taste of bamboo shoot curry, often helps their mother prepare bhaja using mustard oil, but admits their own cooking skill is limited to simple stir-frying and occasional experimentation with herbal teas, and they love the aroma of fresh coriander in every dish.
payload.persona: Bansidhar Ekka is a methodical yet curious 19-year-old who blends traditional forest-craft skills with a drive to modernise village farming, while constantly worrying about whether their plans will materialise, and they often find themselves day-dreaming about a cooperative future.
payload.cultural_background: Bansidhar Ekka belongs to the Santali tribal community of Mayurbhanj district in Odisha. The Santals are traditionally forest dwellers and small-scale farmers who celebrate seasonal festivals such as Sohrai (harvest) and Baha (spring). Their culture emphasizes communal work, oral storytelling, hand-crafted bamboo artefacts, and the distinctive Ol Chiki script used for writing Santali. Living in a rural setting, Bansidhar grows up amidst paddy fields, mango groves and dense forest patches, where community bonds are strong and mutual assistance is a way of life. His methodical nature makes him a reliable participant in organizing village fairs and youth gatherings.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Santali, which he speaks fluently in daily life. Bansidhar has basic familiarity with the Ol Chiki script used for Santali, but owing to his education level being below primary, his reading and writing skills are limited to simple signs and common words. He does not have proficiency in any other language; however, occasional exposure to Odia spoken by neighbours gives him minimal comprehension, though he cannot read or write it.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Bansidhar is skilled in traditional agrarian and craft activities common among the Santali youth of Mayurbhanj. He is adept at cultivating rice and millets, gathering forest produce such as mushrooms and honey, and weaving hand-loom fabrics using locally sourced cotton. He also plays the tamak, a tribal drum, with rhythmic precision, and assists in organizing community festivals and youth gatherings. His practical mindset makes him reliable in managing household chores and providing basic first-aid care to relatives.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Rice and millet cultivation','Forest product gathering (mushrooms, honey)','Handloom weaving using cotton','Playing the tamak drum','Organizing community festivals','Basic first aid','Household management']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bansidhar enjoys a range of communal and creative activities. He loves participating in folk dances such as the Santhali 'Jhumar' during festivals, and frequently plays the tamak alongside friends. He is an active player in village cricket and kabaddi matches, finding energy in team sports. In quieter moments, he reads simple storybooks in Santali, sketches bamboo crafts, and experiments with his mobile phone to learn basic digital skills. Nature walks in the surrounding forests and collecting herbs for traditional remedies also fascinate him.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk dancing (Santhali Jhumar)','Playing the tamak drum','Village cricket','Kabaddi','Reading simple Santali storybooks','Sketching bamboo crafts','Experimenting with mobile phone apps','Nature walks in forests','Collecting medicinal herbs']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bansidhar aspires to bridge his tribal heritage with modern knowledge. He aims to complete his primary schooling through a local adult education program, acquire certification as an agricultural extension worker, and introduce sustainable farming techniques such as organic pest control and water-saving irrigation in his village. Ultimately, he dreams of establishing a small cooperative that sells forest-derived products like honey and woven handicrafts, thereby creating a stable income for his family while preserving cultural traditions. His practical outlook keeps his plans grounded, while his natural curiosity pushes him to explore new methods.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Bansidhar Ekka is a 19-year-old male from a Santali tribal background in rural Mayurbhanj, Odisha, who is unmarried and has below-primary education. He works as an aspiring agricultural extension assistant and speaks Santali fluently, though his reading and writing skills are limited. His lifestyle is centered on traditional forest-craft and farming, with a methodical approach to improving village agriculture.

  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Mayurbhanj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Mayurbhanj
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: Bansidhar Ekka is a 19-year-old male from a Santali tribal background in rural Mayurbhanj, Odisha, who is unmarried and has below-primary education. He works as an aspiring agricultural extension assistant and speaks Santali fluently, though his reading and writing skills are limited. His lifestyle is centered on traditional forest-craft and farming, with a methodical approach to improving village agriculture.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

--- chat ---
Patient: ᱥᱤᱛ ᱥᱨᱤ ᱟᱠᱟᱞ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ ᱥᱟᱦᱮᱵᱽ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱫᱚ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱥᱤᱫᱷᱚᱨ ᱮᱠᱠᱟ, ᱵᱟᱫᱷᱭᱟ ᱟᱹᱛᱩ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱾ ᱯᱟᱪᱷᱮ ᱯᱮ ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱫᱚ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱛᱤ ᱫᱚ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ, ᱯᱟᱞᱮ 100.4°F ᱟᱰᱮᱯᱟᱥᱮ, ᱢᱮᱱᱠᱷᱟᱱ ᱦᱤᱡᱩᱜ ᱥᱮᱱᱚᱜ ᱛᱟᱦᱮᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱥᱟᱶᱛᱮ ᱵᱚᱦᱚᱜ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱦᱚᱸ ᱢᱮᱱᱟᱜᱼᱟ ᱟᱨ ᱦᱚᱲᱢᱚ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱞᱟᱸᱜᱟ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱜ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ, ᱡᱮᱢᱚᱱ ᱫᱤᱱᱟᱹᱢ ᱠᱟᱹᱢᱤ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱫᱟᱲᱮ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱩᱜᱼᱟ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱫᱚ ᱞᱳᱠᱟᱞ ᱫᱳᱠᱟᱱ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱠᱤᱪᱷᱩ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ ᱜᱩᱞᱤᱧ ᱦᱟᱛᱟᱣᱮᱫᱟ, ᱢᱮᱱᱠᱷᱟᱱ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱫᱚ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱠᱚᱢᱚᱜ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱥᱤᱛ ᱥᱨᱤ ᱟᱠᱟᱞ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱥᱤᱫᱷᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣ ᱠᱮᱫᱟ ᱡᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱫᱚ ᱡᱚᱨ, ᱵᱚᱦᱚᱜ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱟᱨ ᱞᱟᱸᱜᱟ ᱠᱷᱟᱹᱛᱤᱨ ᱴᱷᱤᱠ ᱵᱟᱢ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱮᱫᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱫᱚ ᱤᱧ ᱞᱟᱹᱭ ᱫᱟᱲᱮᱭᱟᱜᱼᱟ ᱡᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱴᱷᱮᱱ ᱟᱨ ᱪᱮᱫ ᱞᱚᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱢᱮᱱᱟᱜᱼᱟ? ᱡᱮᱞᱮᱠᱟ, ᱟᱢ ᱴᱷᱮᱱ ᱠᱷᱩᱜ, ᱜᱚᱞᱟ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱥᱮ ᱯᱤᱥᱟᱹᱵᱽ ᱚᱠᱛᱚ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱦᱩᱭᱩᱜᱼᱟ? ᱟᱨ, ᱟᱢ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱦᱚᱲᱢᱚ ᱨᱮ ᱪᱮᱫ ᱫᱷᱟᱵᱽ ᱮᱢ ᱧᱮᱞ ᱟᱠᱟᱫᱟ?
Patient: ᱵᱟᱝ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ, ᱠᱷᱩᱜ ᱥᱮ ᱜᱚᱞᱟ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱩᱜᱼᱟ ᱾ ᱯᱤᱥᱟᱹᱵᱽ ᱨᱮᱦᱚᱸ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱩᱜᱼᱟ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱪᱮᱫ ᱫᱷᱟᱵᱽ ᱦᱚᱸ ᱵᱟᱹᱧ ᱧᱮᱞ ᱟᱠᱟᱫᱟ ᱾ ᱢᱩᱲᱩᱫ ᱟᱱᱟᱴ ᱫᱚ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱟᱨ ᱵᱚᱦᱚᱜ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱚᱠᱟ ᱚᱠᱟ ᱡᱚᱠᱷᱚᱡ ᱜᱚᱞᱟ ᱱᱟᱥᱮ ᱨᱚᱦᱚᱲ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱜ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱠᱷᱮᱛ ᱨᱮ ᱱᱟᱥᱮ ᱛᱟᱲᱟᱢ ᱞᱮᱠᱷᱟᱱ ᱜᱮ ᱤᱧ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱞᱟᱸᱜᱟᱧ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱟ ᱾
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱵᱟᱰᱟᱭ ᱞᱟᱹᱜᱤᱫ ᱥᱟᱨᱦᱟᱣ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱡᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱢᱮᱱᱮᱫᱟ, ᱚᱱᱟ ᱞᱮᱠᱟᱛᱮ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱫᱚ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱥᱟᱫᱷᱟᱨᱚᱱ ᱵᱷᱟᱭᱨᱟᱞ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱞᱮᱠᱟ ᱧᱮᱞᱚᱜ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱚᱨ, ᱵᱚᱦᱚᱜ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱟᱨ ᱦᱚᱲᱢᱚ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱞᱚᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱫᱚ ᱥᱟᱫᱷᱟᱨᱚᱱ ᱜᱮᱭᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ ᱮᱢ ᱦᱟᱛᱟᱣᱮᱫ ᱨᱮᱦᱚᱸ ᱟᱨᱟᱢ ᱵᱟᱢ ᱧᱟᱢᱮᱫ ᱠᱷᱟᱹᱛᱤᱨ, ᱟᱵᱚ ᱫᱚ ᱠᱤᱪᱷᱩ ᱠᱟᱛᱷᱟ ᱵᱷᱟᱹᱵᱤᱛ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱟᱵᱚᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱯᱩᱭᱞᱩ ᱨᱮ, ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱧᱮᱞ ᱢᱮ ᱡᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱫᱟᱜ, ᱳ ᱟᱨ ᱮᱥ ᱥᱮ ᱞᱮᱢᱵᱚ ᱫᱟᱜ ᱮᱢ ᱧᱩᱭᱮᱫᱟ ᱡᱮᱢᱚᱱ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱠᱷᱟᱹᱛᱤᱨ ᱦᱚᱲᱢᱚ ᱨᱮ ᱫᱟᱜ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱚᱵᱷᱟᱵᱽ ᱟᱞᱚ ᱦᱩᱭᱩᱜ ᱢᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱤᱨᱟᱹᱣ ᱫᱚ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱡᱟᱹᱨᱩᱲᱟ, ᱚᱱᱟᱛᱮ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱪᱟᱵᱟᱜ ᱫᱷᱟᱹᱵᱤᱡ ᱠᱮᱴᱮᱡ ᱠᱟᱹᱢᱤ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱥᱟᱦᱟ ᱨᱮ ᱛᱟᱦᱮᱱ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱪᱮᱥᱴᱟ ᱢᱮ ᱾
Patient: ᱴᱷᱤᱠ ᱜᱮᱭᱟ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱛᱤ ᱫᱟᱜ ᱧᱩ ᱟᱨ ᱡᱤᱨᱟᱹᱣ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱪᱮᱥᱴᱟ ᱭᱟ ᱾ ᱢᱮᱱᱠᱷᱟᱱ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱠᱤᱪᱷᱩ ᱫᱤᱱ ᱨᱮᱦᱚᱸ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱪᱟᱵᱟᱜᱼᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱪᱮᱫ ᱤᱧ ᱪᱮᱠᱟ? ᱪᱮᱫ ᱤᱧ ᱟᱥᱯᱟᱛᱟᱞ ᱦᱤᱡᱩᱜ ᱞᱟᱹᱠᱛᱤ?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱫᱚ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱱᱟᱯᱟᱭ ᱠᱩᱠᱞᱤ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱡᱚᱨ 3-4 ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱛᱤ ᱛᱟᱦᱮᱱᱟ, ᱥᱮ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ 102°F ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱪᱮᱛᱟᱱ ᱨᱟᱠᱟᱵᱟ, ᱥᱮ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱟᱢ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱤᱡ ᱮᱢ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱫᱚ ᱟᱥᱯᱟᱛᱟᱞ ᱦᱤᱡᱩᱜ ᱜᱮ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱟᱢᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱨ, ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱦᱚᱴᱚᱜ ᱨᱮ ᱠᱮᱴᱮᱡ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱜᱼᱟ, ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱡᱩᱨ ᱵᱚᱦᱚᱜ ᱦᱟᱹᱥᱩ, ᱥᱮ ᱵᱷᱟᱹᱵᱤᱛ ᱨᱮ ᱟᱱᱟᱴ ᱦᱩᱭᱩᱜᱼᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱫᱚ ᱛᱩᱨᱩᱛ ᱦᱤᱡᱩᱜ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱟᱢᱟ ᱾ ᱱᱤᱛᱚᱜ ᱞᱟᱹᱜᱤᱫ, ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱢᱮ, ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱫᱟᱜ ᱧᱩ ᱢᱮ ᱟᱨ ᱡᱤᱨᱟᱹᱣ ᱢᱮ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱮᱱᱴᱤᱵᱟᱭᱳᱴᱤᱠ ᱤᱧ ᱚᱞ ᱮᱫᱟ, ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱵᱮᱠᱴᱮᱨᱤᱭᱟᱞ ᱤᱱᱯᱷᱮᱠᱥᱚᱱ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ, ᱢᱮᱱᱠᱷᱟᱱ ᱡᱟᱹᱥᱛᱤ ᱠᱟᱭᱛᱮ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱫᱚ ᱵᱷᱟᱭᱨᱟᱞ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾
Patient: ᱴᱷᱤᱠ ᱜᱮᱭᱟ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱥᱟᱨᱦᱟᱣ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱡᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱮᱱᱴᱤᱵᱟᱭᱳᱴᱤᱠ ᱮᱢ ᱚᱞ ᱮᱫᱟ, ᱚᱱᱟ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱧᱩᱛᱩᱢ ᱪᱮᱫ?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱮᱱᱴᱤᱵᱟᱭᱳᱴᱤᱠ ᱫᱚ ᱟᱡᱤᱛᱷᱨᱳᱢᱟᱭᱥᱤᱱ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱯᱮ ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱞᱟᱹᱜᱤᱫ ᱫᱤᱱ ᱨᱮ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱫᱷᱟᱣ 500mg ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱜᱩᱞᱤ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱟᱢᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱢ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱫᱚ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱥᱟᱶ ᱥᱮ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱥᱟᱶ ᱦᱚᱸ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱫᱟᱲᱮᱭᱟᱜᱼᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱨ, ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱡᱚᱨ 100°F ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱪᱮᱛᱟᱱ ᱛᱟᱦᱮᱱᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ 500mg ᱛᱩᱨᱩᱭ ᱜᱷᱚᱱᱴᱟ ᱨᱮ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱫᱷᱟᱣ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱢᱮ ᱾ ᱫᱟᱭᱟ ᱠᱟᱛᱮ ᱮᱱᱴᱤᱵᱟᱭᱳᱴᱤᱠ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱯᱩᱨᱟᱹ ᱠᱳᱨᱥ ᱯᱩᱨᱟᱹᱣ ᱢᱮ ᱾
Patient: ᱤᱧ ᱫᱚ ᱤᱧᱟᱜ ᱟᱹᱛᱩ ᱥᱩᱨ ᱨᱮᱭᱟᱜ ᱯᱷᱟᱨᱢᱮᱥᱤ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱨᱟᱱ ᱤᱧ ᱟᱹᱜᱩᱭᱟ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱫᱚ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ ᱛᱤᱱᱟᱹᱜ ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱦᱩᱭ ᱟᱵᱚᱱᱟ?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱟᱢ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱞᱟᱹᱜᱤᱫ ᱞᱟᱹᱠᱛᱤ ᱞᱮᱠᱟᱛᱮ ᱛᱩᱨᱩᱭ ᱜᱷᱚᱱᱴᱟ ᱨᱮ ᱢᱤᱫ ᱫᱷᱟᱣ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ 500mg ᱡᱚᱢ ᱫᱟᱲᱮᱭᱟᱜᱼᱟ, 24 ᱜᱷᱚᱱᱴᱟ ᱨᱮ ᱡᱟᱹᱥᱛᱤ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱡᱟᱹᱥᱛᱤ 4 ᱫᱷᱟᱣ ᱾ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ ᱥᱟᱶ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱜᱟᱞᱢᱟᱨᱟᱣ ᱠᱟᱛᱮ 3 ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱛᱤ ᱟᱞᱚᱢ ᱡᱚᱢᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ 2-3 ᱢᱟᱦᱟᱸ ᱛᱟᱭᱚᱢ ᱟᱢ ᱱᱟᱯᱟᱭ ᱮᱢ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱯᱮᱨᱟᱥᱤᱴᱟᱢᱳᱞ ᱡᱚᱢ ᱵᱚᱱᱫᱚ ᱫᱟᱲᱮᱭᱟᱜᱼᱟ ᱾ ᱡᱚᱛᱚ ᱠᱷᱚᱱ ᱡᱟᱹᱨᱩᱲᱟᱱ ᱠᱟᱛᱷᱟ ᱫᱚ ᱦᱩᱭᱩᱜ ᱠᱟᱱᱟ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱡᱚᱨ ᱧᱮᱞ ᱾ ᱫᱟᱭᱟ ᱠᱟᱛᱮ ᱫᱤᱱ ᱨᱮ ᱵᱟᱨ ᱫᱷᱟᱣ, ᱥᱮᱛᱟᱜ ᱟᱨ ᱟᱹᱭᱩᱵ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱞᱚᱞᱚ ᱡᱚᱠᱷᱟ ᱢᱮ, ᱟᱨ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱱᱚᱣᱟ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱠᱚᱢᱚᱜᱼᱟ ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱤᱧ ᱞᱟᱹᱭ ᱟᱹᱧ ᱢᱮ ᱾
Patient: ᱴᱷᱤᱠ ᱜᱮᱭᱟ ᱰᱟᱠᱛᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱫᱚ ᱤᱧᱟᱜ ᱞᱚᱞᱚ ᱡᱚᱠᱷᱟ ᱭᱟ ᱟᱨ ᱨᱟᱱ ᱠᱚᱧ ᱡᱚᱢᱟ ᱡᱮᱞᱮᱠᱟ ᱟᱢ ᱢᱮᱱ ᱟᱠᱟᱫᱟ ᱾ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱥᱟᱞᱟ ᱞᱟᱹᱜᱤᱫ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱥᱟᱨᱦᱟᱣ ᱾
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱥᱟᱹᱜᱩᱱ ᱫᱟᱨᱟᱢ ᱵᱟᱹᱱᱥᱤᱫᱷᱚᱨ ᱾ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱠᱷᱮᱭᱟᱞ ᱫᱚᱦᱚᱭ ᱢᱮ ᱾ ᱟᱹᱰᱤ ᱫᱟᱜ ᱧᱩ ᱢᱮ, ᱡᱤᱨᱟᱹᱣ ᱢᱮ ᱟᱨ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱞᱚᱞᱚ ᱡᱚᱠᱷᱟ ᱧᱮᱞ ᱢᱮ ᱾ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱟᱢᱟᱜ ᱟᱨ ᱪᱮᱫ ᱠᱩᱠᱞᱤ ᱛᱟᱦᱮᱱᱟ ᱥᱮ ᱡᱩᱫᱤ ᱟᱢ ᱵᱟᱹᱲᱤᱡ ᱮᱢ ᱵᱩᱡᱷᱟᱹᱣᱟ, ᱛᱚᱵᱮ ᱤᱧ ᱥᱟᱶ ᱵᱟᱝ ᱞᱟᱡᱟᱣ ᱠᱟᱛᱮ ᱟᱨᱦᱚᱸ ᱡᱚᱜᱟᱡᱚᱜᱽ ᱢᱮ ᱾ ᱤᱧ ᱟᱥᱟ ᱭᱟ ᱡᱮ ᱟᱢ ᱞᱚᱜᱚᱱ ᱮᱢ ᱱᱟᱯᱟᱭᱚᱜᱼᱟ ᱾
payload.generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Bansidhar Ekka]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438123456]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|bansidhar.ekka@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mayurbhanj District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor saheb. I am Bansidhar Ekka from the village of Badhia. I have been having a fever for the last three days. It is not a very high fever, maybe around 100.4°F, but it comes and goes. Along with the fever, I also have a headache and my body feels very weak, like I have no strength to do my daily work. I have been taking some paracetamol tablets from the local shop, but the fever is not going down.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: Namaste Bansidhar. I understand you are not feeling well with fever, headache, and weakness. Can you tell me if you have any other symptoms? For example, do you have a cough, sore throat, or any pain while passing urine? Also, have you noticed any rash on your body?
Patient: No doctor, no cough or sore throat. No pain in urine either. I have not seen any rash. The main problem is the fever and the headache. My throat feels a little dry sometimes. I am getting very tired just by walking a little distance in the field.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: Thank you for the information. Based on what you are saying, it sounds like a common viral fever. The symptoms of fever, headache, and body ache are typical. Since you have been taking paracetamol without much relief, we should consider a few things. First, please make sure you are drinking plenty of fluids like water, ORS, or lemon water to prevent dehydration from the fever. Rest is very important, so try to avoid heavy work until the fever comes down.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will try to drink more water and rest. But what if the fever does not go away in a few more days? Should I come to the hospital?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: That is a good question. If the fever persists for more than 3-4 days, or if it goes above 102°F, or if you start feeling much worse, then you should definitely come to the hospital. Also, if you develop a stiff neck, severe headache, or confusion, you must come immediately. For now, continue with the paracetamol as prescribed, take plenty of fluids, and get adequate rest. I will prescribe an antibiotic, just in case it is a bacterial infection, but most likely it is viral.
Patient: Okay doctor. Thank you. What is the name of the antibiotic you are prescribing?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: The antibiotic is Azithromycin. You should take one tablet of 500mg once a day for three days. You can take it with or without food. Along with this, continue the paracetamol 500mg every six hours if the fever is above 100°F. Please make sure to complete the full course of the antibiotic.
Patient: I will get the medicine from the pharmacy near my village. How many days should I take the paracetamol?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: You can take the paracetamol 500mg every six hours as needed for the fever, for a maximum of 4 doses in 24 hours. Do not take it for more than 3 days without consulting a doctor again. If you feel better after 2-3 days, you can stop the paracetamol. The most important thing is to monitor your fever. Please check your temperature twice a day, in the morning and evening, and let me know if it is not improving.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will check my temperature and take the medicines as you said. Thank you very much for your advice.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Subrata Patnaik]]: You are welcome, Bansidhar. Take care of yourself. Drink lots of fluids, get enough rest, and monitor your temperature. If you have any more questions or if you feel worse, do not hesitate to contact me again. I hope you feel better very soon.
payload.judge_reasoning: Santali Ol Chiki prose throughout chat; all tags from allow-list; Latin drug/dose/temp strings permitted; 19M viral-fever case matches persona; no length or script issues.

payload.professional_persona: Navjot Singh is a 51‑year‑old horse‑carriage operator who expertly handles his horses, maintains his carriage, navigates rural routes with precision, and offers courteous, community‑focused transport while they plan a tourist‑friendly upgrade and mentor their youngest son.
payload.linguistic_persona: Navjot Singh speaks fluent Punjabi, understands spoken Hindi in market settings, and can recognise simple signs, using practical numeracy to count fares and manage transactions despite being illiterate, and they rely on memory for financial calculations.
payload.sports_persona: Navjot Singh enjoys watching and occasionally playing Kabaddi with village friends, follows the local cricket team passionately, and maintains fitness through daily horse‑riding warm‑ups and occasional community jogging, and they appreciate the camaraderie of team sports.
payload.arts_persona: Navjot Singh loves listening to regional folk songs and devotional shabads, often humming the melodies of singers like Kuldeep Manak, Surinder Kaur and Gurdas Maan, and occasionally watches regional movies featuring actors like Amrish Puri and comedians such as Binnu Dhillon at the village community hall, and they cherish how music connects them to community stories.
payload.travel_persona: Navjot Singh prefers short weekend trips to nearby historic towns like Jalandhar and Patiala, enjoys occasional pilgrimages to the central shrine in his district, and dreams of a modest vacation to the foothills of the Himalayas, balancing travel desires with a limited family budget, and they carefully plan each outing to avoid overspending.
payload.culinary_persona: Navjot Singh enjoys hearty meals such as sarson da saag with makki di roti, butter chicken, fresh homemade lassi, the sweet tang of jalebi after dinner, and often savours a cup of spiced chai with cardamom and ginger during evening gatherings, and they relish sharing these dishes with neighbours at the communal kitchen.
payload.persona: Navjot Singh is a seasoned horse‑carriage driver who blends traditional hospitality with a cautious eye on modern opportunities, jingles with folk tunes, occasionally forgets their medication, and they value community service above profit.
payload.cultural_background: Navjot Singh belongs to the Punjabi rural community of Amritsar, a region steeped in the traditions of the Punjab plains. He lives in a close‑knit village where agriculture, livestock, and seasonal festivals shape daily life. The agrarian ethos of hard work, hospitality (mehman‑nawazi), and a love for Punjabi folk music and dance permeate his upbringing. As a Sikh, his cultural identity is also anchored in the values of seva (selfless service), equality, and strong community cohesion, balancing respect for age‑old customs with a curiosity about newer ways of living.
payload.linguistic_background: Navjot’s mother tongue is Punjabi, which he speaks fluently and uses for all daily interactions. He has no formal education and is illiterate, so he cannot read or write in Punjabi, Hindi, or English. He can recognise simple signs in Punjabi and Hindi and can count money for fare collection, a skill he honed out of practical necessity. He understands spoken Hindi in market settings and during Gurdwara sermons, but he rarely uses it beyond listening. His curiosity drives him to learn basic numeracy and keep up with new traffic regulations, balancing practicality with a willingness to adapt.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Navjot possesses expert horse‑handling skills, including grooming, feeding, and monitoring health. He expertly navigates the rural roads of Amritsar, knows optimal routes for efficiency, and negotiates fares with passengers. He performs basic mechanical repairs on his carriage, handles routine maintenance, and can troubleshoot minor breakdowns. His deep knowledge of local geography, market timings, and seasonal weather patterns helps him plan trips prudently. He also demonstrates strong interpersonal abilities, offering courteous service, resolving conflicts calmly, and engaging in community conversations with a balanced mix of talkativeness and listening.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Horse handling', 'Carriage maintenance', 'Route navigation', 'Fare negotiation', 'Basic mechanical repair', 'Geographical knowledge of Amritsar region', 'Understanding of market timings', 'Seasonal weather awareness', 'Customer service', 'Conflict resolution', 'Community engagement']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Navjot enjoys listening to Punjabi folk music and Sikh shabads, often humming the hymns while tending to his horses. He loves caring for his animals, spending quiet mornings grooming them. Gardening in his small backyard, especially growing seasonal vegetables, provides him with both relaxation and food for his family. He actively participates in Gurdwara seva, helping with kitchen chores and community events. Storytelling, especially recounting local legends (kisse) to younger villagers, is a cherished pastime. Occasionally, he watches Punjabi movies at the village’s modest community hall, appreciating the blend of humor and drama.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Punjabi folk music and Sikh shabads', 'Caring for horses', 'Gardening and growing seasonal vegetables', 'Participating in Gurdwara seva', 'Storytelling of local legends (kisse)', 'Watching Punjabi movies at the village community hall']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Navjot aims to secure a stable income for his family while preserving his traditional livelihood. He plans to upgrade his horse carriage to a more comfortable, tourist‑friendly version to attract visitors interested in heritage rides. He also wishes to train his youngest son as an apprentice, ensuring the skill set passes to the next generation. Additionally, he is exploring options to acquire a small electric auto‑rickshaw, blending modern transport with his practical knowledge of routes and fares. His ambition is to balance respect for tradition with pragmatic adaptation to new economic opportunities.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 51-year-old male horse-carriage driver from rural Punjab, Amritsar, who is currently married and speaks Punjabi as his first language. He has an illiterate education level and works in a traditional occupation. His lifestyle includes daily horse-riding and community-focused activities.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Amritsar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 51
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Horse Carriage Driver
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Amritsar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 51-year-old male horse-carriage driver from rural Punjab, Amritsar, who is currently married and speaks Punjabi as his first language. He has an illiterate education level and works in a traditional occupation. His lifestyle includes daily horse-riding and community-focused activities.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Mumbai Suburban.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Mumbai Suburban
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 18-year-old male from Mumbai, Maharashtra, who is never married and has a middle-level education, is currently retired or a homemaker. He speaks Sindhi and Hindi, and lives in an urban zone. Clinically, he is a competitive artist who experiences stress and has a habit of consuming street food.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Mumbai Suburban.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Mumbai Suburban
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18-year-old male from Mumbai, Maharashtra, who is never married and retired, has completed middle education. He speaks Sindhi and Hindi, and resides in an urban zone. His lifestyle includes occasional stress and frequent consumption of street food.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:

[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

پرائمري هيلٿ سينٽر ريڪارڊ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Ghatkopar]] ڳوٺ [[VILLAGE|Ghatkopar Gaothan]] ضلعو [[DISTRICT|Mumbai Suburban]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]] | چِتا ۽ ننڊ نہ اچڻ

مريضو ॿن هفتن کان چِتا، چِڙچِڙاپڻي ۽ ننڊ نہ اچڻ جي شڪايتن سان آيو آهي. ॿڌائيندو آهي ته ڊپ محسوس ٿي سگهي ٿو ۽ پيشورانہ تربيت جي ڪلاسن ۾ ڌيان لڳائڻ ۾ تڪليف ٿي رهي آهي. هو 18 ساله، اڪلوتو، بے روزگار مرد [[VILLAGE|Ghatkopar Gaothan]]، [[DISTRICT|Mumbai Suburban]] ۾ رهندو آهي. ॿڌائيندو آهي ته خانداني اميدن ۽ مالي مسئلن سبب دٻاءُ وڌي ويو آهي. ॿڌائيندو آهي ته خودڪشيءَ جا خيال، پنهنجي نقصان يا منشيات جو استعمال ڪونھي. ॿڌائيندو آهي ته بک وڌي وئي آهي پر ننڊ جو معيار خراب آهي. هو مڊل اسڪول پاس ڪيل آهي. ڪو به معلوم دائمی بيماري ناهي. پهرين ڪا به نفسياتي بيماريءَ جي تاريخ ناهي. عام حالت سٺي طرح کاڌو کايل، چِتا واري ۽ تعاون ڪندڙ آهي. ٻولڻ جي رفتار ۽ تال عام آهي. موڊ چِتا واري حالت ۾ آهي. ايفيڪٽ چِتا واري حالت ۾ آهي. سوچڻ جو عمل منطقي آهي. سوچ جو مواد منفي آهي پر ڪا به وهم يا وهمي آواز ناهن. سمجهه مناسب آهي. فيصلو مناسب آهي.

تشخيص: 1. چِتا جو بيماري، مخصوص ناهي. 2. ننڊ نہ اچڻ.

علاج ۽ منصوبو:
1. تسلي ۽ صلاح मशवरو ڏنو ويو.
2. ننڊ جي صفائي ۽ دٻاءُ سنڀالڻ جي تکنیکن بابت صلاح मशवरو.
3. سرٽرالين 25mg روزانو شروع ڪي وئي.
4. 2 هفتن ۾ فالو اپ مقرر ڪيو ويو.
5. وڌيڪ جاچ لاء جekar سڌارو ناهي آيو ته ضلعي اسپتال جي نفسياتي OPD ۾ موڪلियो ويو.

رابطو معلومات: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. آشا ورڪر: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Patil]]. BPL ڪارڊ: [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-MH-400077-1234]]. وٽر ID: [[VOTER_ID|MHAB1234567]]. ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]].

[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Anxiety and Insomnia

Patient presents with complaints of increased anxiety, restlessness, and difficulty sleeping for the past two weeks. Reports feeling 'on edge' and having trouble concentrating at his vocational training classes. He is an 18-year-old never married, unemployed male residing in [[VILLAGE|Ghatkopar Gaothan]], [[DISTRICT|Mumbai Suburban]]. He reports increased stress related to family expectations and financial concerns. He denies any suicidal ideation, self-harm, or substance use. He reports increased appetite but poor sleep quality. Mood is anxious. Affect is anxious. Thought process is logical. Thought content is negative but no delusions or hallucinations. Insight is fair. Judgment is fair. No investigations required at this stage. Diagnosis: 1. Anxiety Disorder, unspecified. 2. Insomnia. Treatment and plan: 1. Reassurance and counseling provided. 2. Advised on sleep hygiene and stress management techniques. 3. Started on Sertraline 25mg once daily. 4. Follow-up scheduled in 2 weeks. 5. Referred to District Hospital's Psychiatry OPD for further evaluation if no improvement. Contact details: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. ASHA Worker: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Patil]]. BPL Card: [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-MH-400077-1234]]. Voter ID: [[VOTER_ID|MHAB1234567]]. ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]].

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Mayurbhanj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Mayurbhanj
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: Bansidhar Ekka is a 19-year-old male from a Santali tribal background in rural Mayurbhanj, Odisha, who is never married and has below-primary education. He works as a homemaker and is fluent in Santali, with limited reading and writing skills in the Ol Chiki script. His lifestyle includes traditional forest-craft skills, modernizing farming practices, and occasional participation in village cricket and kabaddi. He is methodically curious and concerned about materializing his plans for agricultural improvement.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Vitals / acuity: BP 120/80, HR 92, RR 18, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Patient appears anxious, agitated, with psychomotor retardation. Alert and oriented x3. No acute distress noted.
Chief complaint: Patient brought by family for increased irritability, social withdrawal, and decreased appetite for past 2 weeks. Family reports patient has stopped participating in village activities and spends most time alone in room. Patient reports feeling "heavy heart" and "no interest in farming" which he previously enjoyed. No history of psychiatric treatment. No known substance use. Family denies any recent trauma or loss. Patient appears fatigued, with poor eye contact. Speech is slow and monosyllabic. Mood appears depressed. Thought process is logical but content shows hopelessness. No suicidal ideation reported. No psychotic symptoms noted. Plan: Admit for observation, initiate psychiatric evaluation, start low-dose antidepressant, provide counseling support.
